questions.question_text: Your training pipeline reads many small files (e.g., millions of images) from an NFS mount and is slow. You see high `%wa` (I/O wait) and inconsistent throughput. Which approach best distinguishes an IOPS bottleneck from a throughput bottleneck and accounts for filesystem cache effects?
options: A. Measure with tools like `iostat -x`/`sar -d` and a targeted benchmark: large sequential reads to test throughput vs many small random reads to test IOPS; repeat reads to see page cache effects, and compare local SSD vs NFS to isolate network/fileserver latency.
B. Only use `df -h`; if disk usage is below 80% then I/O cannot be the bottleneck.
C. Increase swap size; if swap is bigger, NFS reads become faster due to caching.
D. Convert the dataset to a larger batch size in the model; this always eliminates I/O wait regardless of storage.
questions.explanation: Throughput bottlenecks show up when reading large contiguous data; IOPS bottlenecks show up with many small reads/metadata ops. NFS often adds latency and can amplify metadata overhead (lookups, attribute checks). The page cache can mask storage latency on repeated reads, so you need to test cold vs warm cache behavior (and be careful when dropping caches on shared systems). Comparing local storage vs NFS helps isolate whether the bottleneck is the remote filesystem/network or the application access pattern. This is a common ML pain point when datasets are stored as many small files.

questions.question_text:
You build a Python wheel for a custom CUDA extension on Ubuntu 22.04 and it works on that machine, but when you install it on an older enterprise Linux (glibc older), importing the module fails with a dynamic linker error.

Which statement best explains the issue and a common solution for distributing ML Python binaries on Linux?
options: A. The wheel is linked against a newer glibc/libstdc++ than the target has; build using a manylinux-compatible toolchain/container (or provide multiple wheels) to ensure ABI compatibility.
B. Linux requires static linking for Python extensions; rebuild with `-static` to fix all compatibility issues.
C. The failure happens because `pip` does not support binary wheels on older distros; you must use `conda` only.
D. The issue is that CUDA wheels must always use the system CUDA; remove CUDA from the wheel and rely on `/usr/local/cuda`.
questions.explanation: Many Linux binary compatibility issues come from glibc and libstdc++ version requirements baked into compiled extensions. The manylinux standard exists so wheels can run across a wide range of distros by building against an older, compatible baseline in controlled images. For ML, this is especially relevant for custom ops and native deps. CUDA adds complexity: some packages ship CUDA runtime libraries inside wheels (e.g., many PyTorch wheels), while others rely on system CUDA—either way you must manage ABI/driver compatibility carefully.

questions.question_text: A training job intermittently hangs on Linux when using multiprocessing. You suspect it is stuck on a syscall or file lock. Describe a practical debugging workflow using Linux tools to go from symptom to root cause. Mention at least: logs, `ps`/`top`, `/proc`, and one of `strace` or `perf`.
questions.correct_answer: Workflow: (1) Check application logs and timestamps to identify the last completed step and whether all workers reached the same point. (2) Use `ps -ef`, `top`/`htop` to see process states (R/D/S/Z), CPU usage, and whether one worker is consuming CPU or all are idle. (3) Inspect `/proc/<pid>/status` for state, threads, and memory; `/proc/<pid>/fd` to see open files/sockets; `/proc/<pid>/stack` (if permitted) for kernel stack hints; `/proc/locks` for lock contention. (4) Attach `strace -p <pid> -f` to see if it’s blocked on `futex`, `read`, `open`, `connect`, `accept`, etc., and which file descriptor/path is involved; for system-wide view use `strace -ff -p <parent>` or start the job under `strace`. (5) If it’s CPU-bound but slow, use `perf top`/`perf record` to see hotspots (e.g., contention, page faults, memcpy). (6) Correlate findings: e.g., many processes blocked in `futex` suggests a userspace lock/deadlock; blocked in `read` from NFS indicates storage; blocked in `connect` indicates networking. Then mitigate (change start method, reduce threads, fix lock ordering, move data local, etc.).
questions.explanation: This assesses whether the candidate can debug production issues rather than just write code. `/proc` provides ground truth about process state and resources. `strace` tells you what the process is waiting on at the syscall level (extremely useful for hangs). `perf` helps when the program is running but underperforming. The goal is a repeatable method, not memorized commands.

questions.question_text: You run CPU-based training/inference and notice severe slowdown when using both multi-threaded BLAS (MKL/OpenMP) and DataLoader workers. Which of the following is the best explanation and first fix to try?
options: A. Oversubscription: each worker process spawns many BLAS/OpenMP threads, causing too many runnable threads; first try setting `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`/`MKL_NUM_THREADS=1` (or reducing them) when using many workers.
B. The Linux scheduler cannot schedule Python processes efficiently; first try switching the filesystem to XFS.
C. The slowdown is due to swap usage; first try increasing `ulimit -n`.
D. The slowdown is because `fork` disables SIMD; first try recompiling Python with `-O3`.
questions.explanation: In ML stacks, it’s common to have process-level parallelism (DataLoader workers) and thread-level parallelism (BLAS/OpenMP). If you have N workers and each uses T threads, you can easily create N×T runnable threads, leading to context switching, cache thrash, and worse throughput. A standard fix is to cap BLAS threads (often to 1) when using many workers, or reduce worker count and use fewer, larger batches. CPU affinity/pinning can further help but the first lever is thread counts.

questions.question_text:
You see the following on a Linux box running inference:

- `top` shows the Python process at ~40 GB VIRT but only ~6 GB RES
- The model file is memory-mapped (e.g., via `mmap` or libraries that use it)

What does this likely indicate about memory usage, and why might VIRT be large without equivalent RSS?
questions.correct_answer: A large VIRT with much smaller RSS often indicates the process has a large virtual address space reserved/mapped but not physically resident. With `mmap` of a big model file, the address range is mapped into the process (increasing VIRT), but pages are only faulted into RAM when accessed (demand paging). Unused parts remain non-resident, so RSS stays smaller. Additionally, file-backed mappings can be served from the page cache and may be reclaimed under pressure. This differs from anonymous allocations (e.g., `malloc`/PyTorch tensors) which contribute to RSS when touched.
questions.explanation: ML engineers frequently misinterpret VIRT as “memory used.” Understanding RSS vs VIRT and mmap helps debug “OOM” reports and capacity planning, especially for large model weights, KV caches, and file-backed embeddings.

questions.question_text:
You are packaging an ML model and its runtime on a Linux host (not containerized) and need to decide where to place:
1) a read-only model artifact (~2GB)
2) a writable cache (tokenizers, HF cache, compiled kernels)
3) a service config file

Which mapping best follows Linux filesystem conventions?
options: A. Model in `/opt/myapp/models`, cache in `/var/cache/myapp` (or `/var/lib/myapp`), config in `/etc/myapp/config.yaml`
B. Model in `/tmp/models`, cache in `/usr/local/bin/cache`, config in `/var/log/myapp.conf`
C. Model in `/etc/models`, cache in `/etc/cache`, config in `/opt/myapp/config.yaml`
D. Model in `/var/log/models`, cache in `/var/log/cache`, config in `/usr/local/etc/config.yaml`
questions.explanation: `/opt` is commonly used for add-on application bundles and large read-only assets. Writable, machine-local state belongs under `/var` (`/var/cache` for regenerable caches, `/var/lib` for persistent app state). Configuration belongs in `/etc`. For ML deployments, keeping large models out of `/tmp` avoids accidental eviction and aligns with backup/permissions practices.

questions.question_text: Explain the difference between `bind()` and `listen()` in a typical TCP server, and where a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx) fits in front of a Python model server. Include one practical reason you might put Nginx in front of Uvicorn/Gunicorn for ML inference.
questions.correct_answer: `bind()` associates a socket with a local IP/port (e.g., 0.0.0.0:8000). `listen()` marks the bound TCP socket as a passive socket that will accept incoming connections, creating a backlog queue. The server then calls `accept()` to get per-connection sockets. A reverse proxy like Nginx binds to the public port (often 80/443), terminates TLS, handles client connection management, and forwards requests to the upstream Python server (Uvicorn/Gunicorn) on an internal port. Practical reasons for ML inference: TLS termination and HTTP/2, buffering/timeout control for long inferences, request size limits, rate limiting, better logging, and load balancing across multiple worker processes/hosts.
questions.explanation: Candidates often can run a server but don’t understand why it fails under real traffic. Knowing `bind` vs `listen` clarifies why a service can start yet be unreachable or why port conflicts occur. Reverse proxies are common in production ML serving to provide TLS, stability, and traffic management that Python app servers typically shouldn’t handle alone.

questions.question_text:
You run a FastAPI app inside a Linux VM:

```bash
uvicorn app:api --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000
```

From your laptop, you cannot reach it via the VM's IP. Which change is most appropriate and why?
options: A. Use `--host 0.0.0.0` so the server binds to all interfaces, not just loopback.
B. Use `--host localhost` because it resolves to the VM's public IP.
C. Use `--port 80` because only privileged ports are reachable remotely.
D. Use `--host ::1` because IPv6 loopback is routable from outside the VM.
questions.explanation: Binding to `127.0.0.1` (loopback) makes the service accessible only from within the VM. Binding to `0.0.0.0` listens on all IPv4 interfaces, allowing external connections (subject to firewall/security groups). This is a common ML-serving pitfall when testing model servers in VMs/containers.

questions.question_text:
You have a Python script `serve.py` that starts a model server. You `chmod +x serve.py` and run `./serve.py`, but you get an error like `Exec format error`.

What is the most likely cause and the correct fix?
options: A. The script is missing a shebang line (e.g., `#!/usr/bin/env python3`), so the kernel doesn't know which interpreter to use.
B. The executable bit should not be set for scripts; only binaries can be executable.
C. The file must be located in `/usr/local/bin` to be executable.
D. You need to run `ldconfig` so the dynamic loader can find Python.
questions.explanation: `Exec format error` commonly occurs when the kernel tries to execute a file as a binary but it isn't a valid executable format (e.g., not ELF), and there is no shebang (`#!`) to tell the kernel which interpreter should run it. Adding `#!/usr/bin/env python3` (or an absolute path to python) at the top fixes it. This comes up in ML deployments when shipping entrypoint scripts in containers or on servers.

questions.question_text:
When you run `train` in a shell (e.g., `train --config cfg.yaml`), which of the following best describes what Linux does to decide what gets executed?

Assume `train` is not a shell builtin or alias.
options: A. The shell searches directories in `$PATH` for a file named `train`, checks it is executable, then `execve()` is called; the kernel uses the file's ELF header or shebang (`#!`) to choose the loader/interpreter.
B. The kernel searches directories in `$PATH` and then invokes the dynamic linker to locate `train` and run it.
C. The shell reads `/etc/ld.so.conf` to locate `train`, then runs it with `/bin/sh` unless it is an ELF binary.
D. `train` must be in the current directory unless `$PWD` is in `$PATH`; otherwise Linux refuses to run it for security reasons.
questions.explanation: PATH resolution is performed by the shell (or by library helpers like `execvp`), not by the kernel. After a candidate path is found, the file's executable bit is checked. The shell then calls `execve()`. The kernel inspects the file: if it's an ELF binary, it uses the ELF program interpreter (often `/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2`) to load it; if it starts with `#!`, the kernel runs the specified interpreter (e.g., `/usr/bin/python3`) with the script path as an argument. This matters in ML when wrappers, entrypoints, and venv/conda shims behave differently depending on PATH and shebangs.

questions.question_text:
You are training a PyTorch model on Linux and use a DataLoader with multiple workers:

```python
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

loader = DataLoader(ds, num_workers=8)
for batch in loader:
    train_step(batch)
```

On Linux, the default multiprocessing start method is often `fork`. What practical issues can `fork` cause in ML training, and what are common mitigations?
questions.correct_answer: With `fork`, the child process inherits the parent's address space and thread state. In ML stacks this can cause: (1) deadlocks or hangs when forking a multithreaded process (e.g., MKL/OpenMP threads, CUDA runtime threads) because only the calling thread survives in the child but locks may remain held; (2) CUDA-related errors/crashes because CUDA contexts are not fork-safe; (3) surprising memory growth: copy-on-write can be defeated when children write to inherited pages (e.g., dataset transforms, Python refcount updates), leading to duplicated RSS; (4) file descriptor and RNG state inheritance issues. Mitigations: use `spawn` or `forkserver` start methods (`torch.multiprocessing.set_start_method('spawn', force=True)`), move CUDA initialization into the worker or after worker start, reduce thread counts (`OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`, `MKL_NUM_THREADS=1`) when using many workers, use persistent workers carefully, prefetching tweaks, and consider shared memory/mmap for read-only datasets to benefit from page cache sharing.
questions.explanation: This tests understanding of Linux process semantics as they affect Python ML. `fork` is fast because it uses copy-on-write, but it is unsafe with many libraries that create threads or manage GPU contexts. The key is that the child inherits a snapshot of the parent's memory and resources, but only one thread continues executing—so locks and background threads can be left in inconsistent states. Using `spawn` starts a fresh interpreter process, avoiding inherited thread/CUDA state at the cost of higher startup overhead. Thread oversubscription and memory duplication are common real-world symptoms.

questions.question_text:
A PyTorch DataLoader with many workers fails with errors like "Too many open files" or the inference server starts failing to accept new connections under load.

What Linux limit is commonly involved, how do you inspect it, and how can you raise it for:
1) your current shell session,
2) a systemd service?

Also mention one other related limit that can bite multiprocessing-heavy ML workloads.
questions.correct_answer:
The common limit is the per-process open file descriptor limit (ulimit -n). Inspect with: ulimit -n (shell) or cat /proc/<pid>/limits for a running process. Raise for current shell: ulimit -n 65535 (within allowed hard limit) or adjust /etc/security/limits.conf and PAM limits for persistence. For a systemd service: set LimitNOFILE=65535 (or higher) in the unit file (or a drop-in), then systemctl daemon-reload and restart.
Another related limit: process/thread count limits (ulimit -u / RLIMIT_NPROC) and container pids.max; these can prevent spawning DataLoader workers or threads in BLAS/OpenMP, leading to mysterious hangs/errors.
questions.explanation: ML workloads create many FDs: dataset shards, mmap files, sockets, pipes between worker processes. When FD limits are low (common in services/containers), you’ll see sporadic failures at scale. Strong candidates know how to check both interactive and systemd contexts, and they connect multiprocessing-heavy ML to RLIMIT_NPROC/pids limits as a second common bottleneck.

questions.question_text:
A containerized inference server is slow and occasionally gets OOM-killed even though the node has free CPU. You inspect cgroup limits.

Which mapping between cgroup settings and observed behavior is correct?
options: A. CPU quota (cpu.max / CFS quota) can cap CPU time causing throttling; memory.max can trigger OOM kill inside the cgroup; pids.max can prevent creating new worker processes/threads; cpuset can restrict which CPUs are usable
B. cpu.shares guarantees a hard CPU limit; memory.max only affects swap; pids.max only limits open files; cpuset only affects GPU selection
C. memory.max increases available memory by enabling overcommit; cpu.max only affects I/O throughput; pids.max controls network sockets; cpuset controls filesystem permissions
D. cgroups are only used by systemd, not by containers, so they cannot explain container performance issues
questions.explanation: This checks container resource isolation literacy. CPU quota is a hard cap leading to throttling even if the node is idle; shares are relative weights under contention. memory.max is a hard limit and can cause OOM kills within the cgroup. pids.max can break dataloaders or gunicorn/uvicorn workers by preventing new processes/threads. cpuset restricts CPU affinity and can hurt performance if set too narrowly.

questions.question_text:
A distributed PyTorch job in containers times out during rendezvous or NCCL initialization. Sometimes it works; sometimes you see errors like "connect() timed out", "connection reset", or hangs when scaling up.

List 3 Linux/networking-level causes that can produce these symptoms and one check/fix for each. Your answers should be practical (commands/settings) and ML-relevant (multi-node, many connections).
questions.correct_answer:
Possible causes + checks/fixes:
1) Firewall / security group / iptables blocking required ports (master port, ephemeral return ports, or NCCL ports). Check: nc -vz <master> <port>, ss -lntp/ss -antp, inspect firewall rules (iptables -S / nft list ruleset) and cloud SGs. Fix: open required ports; explicitly set MASTER_PORT; restrict/define NCCL port range if needed.
2) Ephemeral port exhaustion or conntrack table exhaustion under many short-lived connections. Check: cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range, ss -s, conntrack -S (or /proc/sys/net/netfilter/nf_conntrack_count vs nf_conntrack_max). Fix: widen ephemeral port range, increase nf_conntrack_max, reduce connection churn, reuse connections.
3) MTU mismatch / jumbo frames issues causing drops/blackholing (often shows as hangs/timeouts). Check: ping -M do -s <size> to test PMTU, ip link show for MTU, look for drops in ethtool -S. Fix: align MTU across hosts/VPC, disable jumbo if misconfigured, ensure PMTUD works (ICMP allowed).
Other acceptable causes include DNS issues, asymmetric routing, TCP backlog limits, or incorrect interface selection (set NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME).
questions.explanation: Distributed training failures are frequently infra/network issues rather than ML code bugs. Strong candidates can map symptoms (timeouts/hangs under scale) to Linux primitives: ports, conntrack, MTU/PMTU, interface binding, and firewalling. The best answers include concrete commands and a remediation strategy.

questions.question_text:
You’re deploying a Python ML inference API on a GPU server and want it to run under systemd.

Write (or describe) a minimal systemd unit setup that:
- runs as a non-root user,
- loads environment variables from an EnvironmentFile,
- restarts on failure with backoff,
- logs to journald (default),
- supports a graceful reload if the app handles SIGHUP.

Also mention one common pitfall related to PATH/virtualenv/conda under systemd.
questions.correct_answer:
Example unit (/etc/systemd/system/ml-infer.service):

[Unit]
Description=ML Inference Service
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=mlsvc
Group=mlsvc
WorkingDirectory=/opt/ml-infer
EnvironmentFile=/etc/ml-infer/ml-infer.env
ExecStart=/opt/ml-infer/venv/bin/python -m uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=60
StartLimitBurst=5

# Optional hardening/limits
# LimitNOFILE=65535

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Pitfall: systemd services do not run with your interactive shell init, so PATH/conda activation doesn’t happen. You must use absolute paths (e.g., venv python) or explicitly set Environment=PATH=... and other vars (CUDA paths, HF_HOME, etc.).
questions.explanation: ML engineers are often expected to operationalize inference. This question checks that you can translate requirements into systemd primitives: EnvironmentFile for secrets/config, Restart policies for resilience, journald logging via stdout/stderr, and ExecReload for graceful reloads. The PATH/conda pitfall is a common real incident source.

questions.question_text:
A training job suddenly starts failing with CUDA errors after a driver update. Application logs are vague, but you suspect a kernel/driver issue.

Which command is most directly useful to check kernel-side messages (driver resets, GPU Xid errors, OOM killer, IOMMU faults) that won’t necessarily appear in your Python logs?
options: A. dmesg (or journalctl -k) to view kernel ring buffer / kernel logs
B. cat ~/.bashrc to check environment variables
C. ls -la to verify file permissions
D. pip freeze to list Python dependencies
questions.explanation: This tests the kernel/userspace boundary: Python logs capture userspace exceptions, but GPU driver faults, kernel OOM kills, and device resets are recorded by the kernel. dmesg or journalctl -k is the first place to look when the failure mode smells like driver/kernel behavior rather than application logic.

questions.question_text:
You see intermittent "Permission denied" errors for temporary files when multiple users run jobs on the same machine. Someone suggests "fixing" it by removing the sticky bit from /tmp.

What is the sticky bit on /tmp for, and what would removing it break?
questions.correct_answer: The sticky bit on a world-writable directory like /tmp (typically permissions drwxrwxrwt) ensures that users can only delete/rename files they own (or that root owns), even though everyone can create files there. Removing the sticky bit would allow any user to delete or rename other users’ files in /tmp, creating security and reliability problems (e.g., deleting another user’s temp files, breaking running jobs, enabling certain symlink/race attacks). The right fix is to keep the sticky bit and instead debug ownership/permissions, umask, or use per-user temp dirs (e.g., $TMPDIR) and correct service user settings.
questions.explanation: This checks that candidates understand a foundational Linux safety mechanism that directly affects ML workloads (lots of temp files: dataloaders, compilation caches, torch extensions). The correct operational response is to preserve sticky-bit semantics and fix the actual permission/ownership issue.

questions.question_text:
On a shared multi-user GPU server, a team stores datasets under /data/shared. New files created by one user are not writable by others in the same Unix group, causing training failures.

Which approach best solves this while avoiding world-writable permissions?
options: A. Set directory group ownership and setgid bit, and use a collaborative umask: chgrp -R ml /data/shared; chmod 2775 /data/shared; ensure users have umask 002 (or ACLs) so new files are group-writable
B. chmod -R 777 /data/shared so everyone can read/write; it’s simplest and safe on internal networks
C. Only add users to the sudoers file so they can edit files as root
D. Move datasets into /tmp because /tmp is shared by everyone
questions.explanation: The setgid bit on a directory (2xxx) forces new files/directories to inherit the directory’s group, which is ideal for shared datasets. Combined with a group-friendly umask (002) or default ACLs, collaborators can write without making the directory world-writable. This is a common real-world ML ops issue on shared GPU boxes.

questions.question_text:
An inference service is behaving oddly on one host: it keeps re-downloading model weights and rebuilding CUDA kernels every restart. You want to quickly inspect common Linux locations to confirm where configs, logs, and caches live.

Name typical locations for:
1) system-wide configuration,
2) system logs,
3) temporary files,
4) per-user caches,
5) locally installed software under /usr/local,
6) common ML caches (pip/conda/torch/huggingface).

Give example paths.
questions.correct_answer: 1) System-wide configuration: /etc (e.g., /etc/systemd/system/*.service, /etc/environment, /etc/profile.d/*). 2) System logs: /var/log (e.g., /var/log/syslog or /var/log/messages, /var/log/journal for persistent journald). 3) Temporary files: /tmp (often cleared on reboot; sticky bit), and sometimes /var/tmp (more persistent). 4) Per-user caches: ~/.cache (XDG_CACHE_HOME), e.g., ~/.cache/pip, ~/.cache/torch, ~/.cache/huggingface. 5) Locally installed software: /usr/local/bin, /usr/local/lib, /usr/local/cuda (common in CUDA installs). 6) ML caches: pip: ~/.cache/pip; conda: ~/miniconda3/pkgs or ~/.conda/pkgs (and envs under ~/miniconda3/envs); PyTorch extensions: ~/.cache/torch_extensions and ~/.cache/torch; Hugging Face: ~/.cache/huggingface (or HF_HOME), datasets under ~/.cache/huggingface/datasets; also NVIDIA kernels/build caches sometimes under ~/.nv or ~/.cache.
questions.explanation: This tests practical debugging instincts: repeated downloads/compilations are often cache-path or permission issues. Knowing where Linux 'things live' lets you quickly check disk usage, permissions, and whether a service user (systemd) has a different HOME and thus a different cache directory.

questions.question_text:
You have a Dockerfile that works on Ubuntu (apt) but fails on a RHEL/CentOS-based image when installing dependencies for an ML build.

Which statement best captures the practical difference and the likely fix?
options: A. yum/dnf use RPM repos; package names differ (e.g., python3-devel vs python3-dev, gcc-c++ vs g++). You must use dnf/yum and the distro-specific package names/repositories (EPEL/PowerTools/CRB) as needed
B. yum/dnf cannot install compilers inside containers; you must compile on the host
C. apt installs from source while yum/dnf installs only binaries, so ML builds are impossible on RHEL-based images
D. The only fix is to switch the base image to Ubuntu because CUDA does not work on RHEL-based distros
questions.explanation: AI/ML engineers often troubleshoot container builds across distros. The core issue is repository ecosystem and package naming: Debian/Ubuntu use apt with -dev names; RHEL family uses dnf/yum with -devel and often requires enabling additional repos (EPEL, CRB/PowerTools) to get development packages. The underlying concept is the same: install compilers + headers, but with distro-appropriate tooling and repos.

questions.question_text:
You are on Ubuntu and a pip install of a package with a native extension fails (e.g., tokenizers, xformers, or a custom CUDA extension) with errors like:
- "fatal error: Python.h: No such file or directory"
- "gcc: command not found"
- "fatal error: cuda.h: No such file or directory"

Which set of actions is the most appropriate first pass to fix this on Ubuntu?
options: A. Install build tools and headers: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y build-essential python3-dev linux-headers-$(uname -r) (and CUDA toolkit/dev packages if needed), then retry
B. Run sudo pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel and retry; compilers are not needed for ML packages
C. Reboot the machine to refresh /usr/include and retry the build
D. Delete ~/.cache/pip and retry; missing headers are usually cache corruption
questions.explanation: Native extensions need a compiler toolchain and the right development headers. On Ubuntu, build-essential provides gcc/g++/make; python3-dev provides Python.h; linux-headers is often needed for kernel-module-adjacent builds or some drivers; CUDA-related headers come from CUDA dev packages/toolkit. Upgrading pip can help packaging issues but won’t create missing headers or compilers.

questions.question_text: Your training job’s RSS (resident memory) looks low right after starting N DataLoader workers with fork, but then memory usage steadily climbs and the host OOMs. Explain how Linux copy-on-write (CoW) after fork can lead to this, and name two coding patterns that commonly trigger CoW page breaks in ML pipelines.
questions.correct_answer: After fork(), the parent and child initially share the same physical pages marked copy-on-write. RSS can look deceptively small because many pages are shared. When either process writes to a shared page (even a small write), the kernel must copy that page so each process gets its own private copy, increasing total memory. In ML pipelines, common CoW triggers include: (1) modifying large in-memory datasets or numpy arrays/tensors that were created in the parent before forking (e.g., caching decoded samples, writing into a shared numpy buffer), and (2) Python runtime activity that writes to previously shared pages such as reference count updates, object mutations, logging buffers, or touching large globals (e.g., a big dict/list cache) in each worker. Mitigations include using spawn/forkserver, constructing large buffers inside workers, using memory-mapped read-only data, and avoiding parent-side preloading of huge mutable objects before forking.
questions.explanation: CoW is a performance optimization, not a guarantee of memory savings. Forking a process with a large heap is cheap until writes happen. ML workloads often have large parent heaps (tokenizers, cached datasets, model weights, numpy arrays). Small per-worker writes can explode memory because they duplicate 4KB pages across workers. Recognizing CoW-driven OOM helps you choose architectural fixes (spawn, mmap, worker-local initialization) rather than chasing 'leaks' in the usual sense.

questions.question_text:
You’re debugging a PyTorch training job on Linux. The DataLoader uses multiple workers and the job sometimes hangs or crashes only on one host. You suspect it’s related to process creation.

Explain, at a high level:
1) process vs thread in Linux (what’s shared vs isolated),
2) fork vs exec, and
3) why PyTorch’s multiprocessing start methods (fork/spawn/forkserver) can change behavior (especially with CUDA and large parent-process state).

Include one concrete mitigation you’d try first.
questions.correct_answer:
1) A Linux process has its own virtual address space and resources like file-descriptor table entries, while threads are multiple execution contexts within one process that share the same address space/heap and most process resources (FDs, mappings), differing mainly in registers/stack and some thread-local state. 2) fork() creates a new process by duplicating the parent’s address space and metadata (initially via copy-on-write), while exec() replaces the current process image with a new program, keeping some attributes (e.g., PID, some FDs depending on CLOEXEC). 3) In PyTorch DataLoader, 'fork' inherits the parent’s Python interpreter state, imported modules, open FDs, locks, and (dangerously) CUDA runtime/context state; forking after CUDA initialization can deadlock or crash. 'spawn' starts a fresh Python process and imports the main module, avoiding inheriting unsafe state at the cost of overhead and requiring picklable objects. 'forkserver' forks from a clean server process to reduce inherited state compared to forking the training process.
Mitigation: switch DataLoader/multiprocessing start method to 'spawn' (or 'forkserver') and ensure CUDA initialization happens only in the main process (e.g., set start method early, avoid touching CUDA before workers start).
questions.explanation: Interviewers want to see you connect Linux primitives to ML symptoms. DataLoader workers are processes by default; with 'fork' they inherit a snapshot of the parent (copy-on-write) including Python runtime state. That can break when the parent already created threads (common in BLAS, dataloading libs) or initialized CUDA. 'spawn' is safer because it avoids inheriting that state, but requires code to be import-safe (guard with if __name__ == '__main__':) and objects to be picklable. A practical first step is forcing spawn/forkserver and moving CUDA initialization out of worker processes.

questions.question_text: On a dual-socket NUMA server with 8 GPUs, you notice dataloader CPU usage is high and GPU utilization is uneven. You suspect cross-socket memory traffic. What practical Linux actions would you take to test and mitigate NUMA issues for the training job?
questions.correct_answer: Test: inspect NUMA topology `lscpu` and `numactl --hardware`; check per-NUMA memory/CPU usage with `numastat -p <pid>`; verify GPU/PCIe locality (often via `nvidia-smi topo -m`). Mitigate: pin CPU and memory allocations to the NUMA node local to the GPUs used by a process, e.g., launch per-GPU/per-rank workers with `numactl --cpunodebind=<node> --membind=<node> ...` (or `--physcpubind`), and align dataloader worker threads to the same socket (taskset/cpuset). Ensure each DDP rank is bound to CPUs close to its GPU(s) and avoid oversubscribing cores. Re-test by comparing GPU util, step time, and `numastat` remote memory stats before/after.
questions.explanation: NUMA effects are common in multi-GPU servers: if dataloader threads run on one socket while memory is allocated on the other, or if a GPU is attached to a different socket than the CPU feeding it, you can get high latency and reduced throughput. Pinning CPU and memory locality reduces cross-socket traffic and stabilizes per-GPU performance.

questions.question_text: A training job runs inside a container and shows 100% CPU usage, but throughput is low and `top` on the host shows the machine is mostly idle. You suspect CPU throttling due to cgroups. Which files/commands best confirm CPU quota throttling from inside the container?
options: A. `cat /proc/cpuinfo` only; it shows the quota directly
B. Check cgroup CPU settings and stats: `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.max` (or `cpu.cfs_quota_us`/`cpu.cfs_period_us`) and `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.stat` for throttling counters/time
C. `lsmod | grep cgroup` to see if cgroups are enabled
D. `ping -c 1 localhost` to test CPU speed
questions.explanation: Containers can be limited by cgroup CPU quotas. `cpu.max` (cgroup v2) or CFS quota/period (v1) tells you the configured limit, and `cpu.stat` reveals whether the workload is being throttled (throttled periods/time increasing). This explains why a process can appear “busy” within its quota while the host remains idle.

questions.question_text: You’re optimizing an ML data pipeline reading millions of small files (e.g., JPEGs) from a local SSD. Training shows high CPU sys time and poor throughput. Which change is most likely to improve performance on Linux without changing the model, and why?
options: A. Disable the page cache globally so every read hits disk
B. Pack samples into larger shard files (e.g., tar/TFRecord/WebDataset) to reduce metadata ops and seek overhead; optionally tune readahead and prefetch to match access patterns
C. Use `cat` to concatenate files at runtime for every batch
D. Force `O_DIRECT` for all reads from Python to bypass the page cache always
questions.explanation: Millions of small files cause heavy metadata overhead (open/stat/close), directory traversal costs, and fragmented I/O, often showing up as high system CPU time. Sharding into larger sequential reads dramatically improves throughput by reducing per-file overhead and enabling effective readahead/page cache behavior. `O_DIRECT` can help in specific cases but often hurts and is hard to use correctly from Python; disabling page cache is generally counterproductive.

questions.question_text: A Python ML inference server intermittently fails under load with errors like `OSError: [Errno 24] Too many open files` and sometimes `RuntimeError: can't start new thread`. Which statement best explains what’s happening on Linux and what you should check first?
options: A. It’s a Python bug; reinstall Python and ignore Linux limits

B. These often indicate hitting per-process limits (`ulimit -n` for file descriptors, `ulimit -u`/`nproc` or cgroup `pids.max` for threads/processes); first check `ulimit -a`, `/proc/<pid>/limits`, and if under systemd check the unit’s `LimitNOFILE`/`TasksMax`
C. It’s always caused by low disk space; delete files in /proc
D. It’s always caused by DNS; restart resolvconf
questions.explanation: High-concurrency ML servers can exhaust file descriptors (sockets, files) and thread/process limits. On Linux, threads consume resources and are constrained by ulimits and sometimes cgroup PID limits. Systemd services often have different limits than an interactive shell; checking `/proc/<pid>/limits` and systemd unit settings quickly reveals whether you’re capped by `nofile`, `nproc`, stack, or `TasksMax`/`pids.max`.

questions.question_text: You need to make an ML training run reproducible across two Linux hosts. List the key environment details you would capture (at least 8 items), and give a quick way to diff the two hosts using Linux commands (no special tooling required).
questions.correct_answer: Capture at least: (1) OS distro + version (`/etc/os-release`), (2) kernel version (`uname -a`), (3) GPU driver version (`nvidia-smi`), (4) CUDA toolkit/runtime version (`nvcc --version` and/or `nvidia-smi` CUDA version), (5) cuDNN version (package version or `ldconfig -p | grep cudnn`), (6) NCCL version (`python -c 'import torch; print(torch.cuda.nccl.version())'` or package info), (7) glibc version (`ldd --version`), (8) container image digest if containerized (`docker inspect --format='{{.RepoDigests}}' ...`), (9) Python version and key pip/conda packages (`python -V`, `pip freeze`), (10) ulimits (`ulimit -a`) and relevant sysctls, (11) environment variables affecting performance/repro (e.g., `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`, `NCCL_*`, `OMP_NUM_THREADS`, `MKL_*`), (12) CPU model/flags (`lscpu`) and NUMA topology. Quick diff approach: collect snapshots into files and diff: `ssh hostA 'uname -a; cat /etc/os-release; nvidia-smi; ulimit -a; env | sort' > A.txt` and same for hostB, then `diff -u A.txt B.txt` (or use `comm` on sorted outputs). For libraries, compare `ldd` of the same binary/extension on both hosts.
questions.explanation: Reproducibility failures often come from low-level differences (kernel/driver/libc/CUDA/NCCL) or runtime limits/env vars. Capturing both software and resource-limit context, then diffing text snapshots, is a practical incident-ready method.

questions.question_text: You’re debugging a training server that suddenly started failing with “No space left on device”. The data disk has plenty of space, but the root volume is nearly full. Which Linux filesystem locations are most likely involved, and what is a safe immediate mitigation for ML workloads?
options: A. `/proc` and `/sys` are filling up; delete files under them to free space
B. `/tmp` and `/var/log` are common culprits; identify large files with `du` and rotate/clean logs or redirect temp/checkpoints to a larger mounted volume
C. `/bin` is filling up; remove bash
D. `/etc` is filling up; delete config files
questions.explanation: `/proc` and `/sys` are virtual filesystems and not freed by deleting entries. In ML, root fills up due to large temporary files (`/tmp`), container layers, pip caches, and runaway logs in `/var/log`. Immediate mitigations include cleaning/rotating logs, removing stale temp files, and reconfiguring jobs to write checkpoints/caches to a dedicated data volume (e.g., `/mnt`), plus setting `TMPDIR` to a large disk.

questions.question_text: In a multi-node distributed training run, metrics and logs from different nodes appear out of order, and some workers time out even though networking looks fine. Why can time synchronization (NTP/chrony) matter here, and what symptom strongly suggests clock skew rather than network latency?
options: A. Time sync doesn’t matter; Linux uses monotonic clocks for everything so wall clock can be wrong with no impact

B. Time sync matters because TLS certificates and token expiry depend on wall time; also log ordering/metric timestamps can break; a strong symptom is large differences in `date` across nodes or errors like “certificate not yet valid/expired” and event timestamps in the future/past
C. Time sync matters only for file permissions; a strong symptom is `chmod` failing
D. Time sync matters only on Windows; a strong symptom is blue screens
questions.explanation: Distributed systems often rely on wall-clock time for logging, metrics, token/cert validation, and sometimes timeouts computed from timestamps. Clock skew can make events appear out of order, break correlation, and cause auth failures (cert not yet valid/expired). While monotonic clocks exist, many components still use wall time. A quick indicator is inconsistent `date`/NTP status across nodes and auth/log timestamp anomalies.

questions.question_text: You see crashes on one host but not another when importing a compiled ML extension. The kernel log shows `illegal instruction` and the crash happens immediately in native code. Name two common root causes in ML infra and one Linux-level check/command you’d use for each to confirm.
questions.correct_answer: Common causes: (1) CPU instruction set mismatch (e.g., binary built with AVX/AVX2/AVX-512 but running on older CPU). Check CPU flags: `lscpu` or `grep -m1 flags /proc/cpuinfo` and compare to what the binary expects (sometimes visible via build flags or `objdump -d`/`readelf -A` depending on arch). (2) ABI/library mismatch (e.g., different glibc/libstdc++/CUDA/NCCL versions than the extension was built against). Check dynamic deps: `ldd path/to/extension.so` and compare versions; also check `strings extension.so | grep GLIBC` or `readelf -s` for required symbol versions. Another frequent one is mixing compiler/libstdc++ ABIs across environments. These checks quickly separate “hardware capability” from “runtime library” issues.
questions.explanation: Illegal instruction is often a CPU feature issue (the CPU can’t execute an opcode) or a binary/library mismatch leading to jumping into invalid code paths. ML stacks are prone to this due to prebuilt wheels, heterogeneous fleets, and tight coupling to CUDA/toolchain versions.

questions.question_text: A PyTorch training job crashes with `Segmentation fault (core dumped)` right after importing a custom CUDA extension. On a typical Linux system, where would you look to confirm whether a core dump was generated, and what is a minimal next step to get a high-level stack trace?
options: A. Core dumps are always in the current working directory; run `cat core` to read the stack trace
B. Check `ulimit -c` and `/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern`; then use `coredumpctl info`/`coredumpctl gdb` (systemd) or run `gdb -ex bt -c core <python>` to get a backtrace
C. Check `/etc/passwd` for the extension’s username; then reboot to clear the crash
D. Run `dmesg -C` to clear the kernel ring buffer; the crash will be fixed
questions.explanation: Core dump generation depends on limits (`ulimit -c`) and the kernel core pattern (`/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern`). On systemd-based distros, `coredumpctl` is the standard way to locate and inspect dumps. A minimal next step is to obtain a backtrace in gdb (`bt`) to see whether the crash is in the extension, libc, CUDA, etc. That guides the next hypothesis (ABI mismatch, illegal instruction, null deref, etc.).

questions.question_text: You’re triaging “it’s slow” for a multi-GPU training job in a container. You suspect GPU starvation due to CPU/dataloader or I/O issues. Describe a concrete 5–7 step Linux workflow (commands + what signals you look for) to distinguish: (1) CPU bound dataloader, (2) disk I/O bound reads, (3) network storage bottleneck, (4) GPU underutilization due to host/container limits (cgroups).
questions.correct_answer: Example workflow: (1) Check GPU utilization and kernel time: `nvidia-smi dmon -s pucvmt` or `nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,utilization.memory,pstate,clocks.sm --format=csv -l 1`; look for low GPU util with stable low power/clocks. (2) Check CPU saturation/run queue: `top`/`htop` and `mpstat -P ALL 1`; look for high user%/sys% on dataloader processes, high load with many runnable threads. (3) Check memory pressure/page faults: `free -h`, `vmstat 1`; look for swap in/out (si/so), high `r` run queue, high `wa`, and rising major faults. (4) Check disk I/O latency and saturation: `iostat -xz 1` and `pidstat -d 1`; look for high %util, high await, and the dataloader PID doing lots of reads. (5) If data is on network FS/object store gateway: `sar -n DEV 1` + `ss -s` (retransmits) and possibly `nfsstat -c`/`mount` to confirm NFS; look for NIC saturation, drops, retransmits, or NFS client latency. (6) Check cgroup/container limits: `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.max` (or `cpu.cfs_quota_us`), `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max`, `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/pids.max`, and `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.stat`; look for throttling counters increasing, memory near max, or PID limit hits. (7) Correlate: if GPU util is low while CPU is pegged and I/O wait is low → CPU/dataloader bound; if GPU util is low and `iostat` shows high await/%util → disk bound; if disk is fine but NIC is saturated/retransmits or NFS stats show latency → network storage bound; if host looks idle but container shows throttling/low CPU quota → cgroup constrained.
questions.explanation: The key is to avoid guessing: measure GPU utilization first, then determine whether the pipeline feeding the GPU is limited by CPU, memory, I/O, network, or container quotas. Using a consistent set of commands makes it repeatable and fast in incidents.

questions.question_text: A training run is reported as “it’s slow” on a Linux server. You have SSH access. Which initial triage set of commands is most appropriate to quickly determine whether the bottleneck is CPU, memory pressure, disk I/O, or network (before diving into app-level profiling)?
options: A. `history`, `alias`, `echo $PATH`, `whoami`
B. `top` (or `htop`), `free -m`, `iostat -xz 1`, `sar -n DEV 1` (or `ss -s`/`nload`) to check CPU run queue, memory/swap, disk utilization/await, and network throughput/errors
C. `cat /etc/os-release`, `uname -a`, `lsb_release -a`, `date`
D. `find / -type f | wc -l`, `du -sh /`, `ls -R /var/log`
questions.explanation: A good first pass is structured: CPU (utilization, load average, run queue), memory (available memory, swap-in/out), I/O (device utilization, await/latency, throughput), and network (throughput, drops/retransmits). `top/htop` surfaces CPU usage, load, and per-process state; `free` shows memory pressure; `iostat -xz 1` highlights I/O saturation and latency; `sar -n DEV 1` (or similar) shows network throughput and errors. This narrows the bottleneck quickly before deeper tooling (perf, flamegraphs, app tracing).

questions.question_text: A shared dataset directory is on NFS at /mnt/datasets and model checkpoints are written to /mnt/checkpoints. A teammate ran `chmod -R 777 /mnt/checkpoints` to fix a permission error. Explain why this is risky on Linux in a multi-user ML environment, and propose a safer permission/ownership strategy (include concrete commands).
questions.correct_answer: It’s risky because 0777 makes files world-writable: any user/process can modify/delete checkpoints (integrity risk), plant malicious files (supply-chain risk), or exfiltrate sensitive artifacts if combined with world-readable defaults. It also defeats auditing and can lead to accidental corruption during concurrent runs. Safer approach: use per-project groups and setgid directories so new files inherit group; keep others at 0. Example: create group `mlproj`, add users, set ownership and perms: `chgrp -R mlproj /mnt/checkpoints && chmod 2770 /mnt/checkpoints` (setgid bit), optionally apply ACLs: `setfacl -m g:mlproj:rwx /mnt/checkpoints` and default ACL `setfacl -d -m g:mlproj:rwx /mnt/checkpoints`. For per-run isolation, write to user-owned subdirs with 0700 or 0750. Also set a sane umask (e.g., 027) so new files aren’t world-readable by default.
questions.explanation: ML artifacts (checkpoints, tokenizer files, configs) are effectively executable inputs to later jobs. World-writable directories allow tampering and accidental overwrites. Using groups + setgid/ACLs provides shared access without opening access to all users. A correct umask prevents accidental world-readable tokens or model artifacts.

questions.question_text: You’re setting up a training job that needs access to an S3 bucket (or similar object store). The team currently puts the access key in a world-readable config file inside a shared project directory. What is the best Linux-appropriate approach that follows least privilege and reduces accidental leakage on a multi-user GPU box?
options: A. Store the key in /tmp/creds.txt with mode 0644 so every process can read it reliably
B. Put the key in an environment variable and export it in /etc/profile so it’s available to all users

C. Use a per-service/per-user credential file with restrictive permissions (e.g., 0600), owned by the runtime user, and scope the IAM policy to only required bucket/prefix; avoid baking secrets into images and logs
D. Commit the key to the repo but encrypt the repository with a password
questions.explanation: Least privilege is both OS-level and cloud/IAM-level: run the job as a dedicated user/service account, restrict the credential file to that user (0600), and scope the token/policy to the minimal bucket/prefix/actions. Avoid world-readable locations (shared dirs, /tmp with loose perms), avoid system-wide exports (/etc/profile), and avoid committing secrets. In practice, use a secret manager or mounted secret file in containers; ensure logs don’t print env vars.

questions.question_text: A PyTorch DDP job (NCCL backend) intermittently hangs during initialization on a multi-node cluster. Sometimes you see timeouts; other times it works. Which Linux/networking issue is a realistic root cause, and what is a practical mitigation?
options: A. The nodes have different hostnames; fix by editing `/etc/hosts` only
B. Ephemeral port exhaustion or conntrack/NAT table limits dropping connections; mitigate by increasing ephemeral port range/conntrack limits or avoiding NAT between nodes
C. The GPU clocks are different; fix by setting persistence mode
D. The kernel page size differs; fix by enabling hugepages
questions.explanation: DDP/NCCL can open many TCP connections (especially with many ranks), and unstable behavior can come from ephemeral port exhaustion or conntrack/NAT limits (common in cloud or firewalled environments). Symptoms include intermittent hangs/timeouts during rendezvous or communicator setup. Mitigations include increasing `net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range`, raising `nf_conntrack_max`, ensuring stateful firewalls/NAT aren’t in the data path, and tuning NCCL interface selection (e.g., `NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME`) and timeouts where appropriate.

questions.question_text:
You deploy a Python model server as a systemd service. It restarts in a loop and environment variables you set in your shell (e.g., `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`, `MODEL_PATH`) don't appear inside the service. Provide a minimal unit file snippet that:
- runs as user `mlsvc`
- sets two environment variables
- restarts on failure with a backoff
- shows how you'd view logs in journald
questions.correct_answer:
Example unit snippet:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Model Server
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
User=mlsvc
WorkingDirectory=/opt/model-server
Environment="CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0"
Environment="MODEL_PATH=/models/my_model"
ExecStart=/opt/venv/bin/python -m myserver --port 8080
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=60
StartLimitBurst=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Logs:
- `journalctl -u model-server.service -f`
- For recent failures: `journalctl -u model-server.service --since "1 hour ago"`

Explanation of env issue: systemd does not inherit your interactive shell environment by default; you must set `Environment=` (or `EnvironmentFile=`) in the unit or configure it via drop-in.
questions.explanation: ML engineers often own model-serving reliability. Knowing systemd restart semantics and that services don't inherit your shell env prevents many production outages. Journald is the default place to debug crash loops and dependency failures.

questions.question_text: A model server in a container is stable under light load but falls over under stress. You see errors like 'can't start new thread' or 'Resource temporarily unavailable', even though memory is fine and no OOM kills occur. Which cgroup/container limit is a common cause?
options: A. `cpuset.cpus` restricting the container to too few cores
B. `pids.max` limiting the number of processes/threads
C. `blkio.weight` set too low
D. `cpu.shares` set too high
questions.explanation: Thread creation counts against the cgroup PID limit (`pids.max`). Under load, servers may spawn many threads (Python worker pools, gRPC threads, tokenizers, BLAS threads). If `pids.max` is low, you can get failures creating threads/processes without any OOM. CPU and IO controls change performance, but they don't typically produce 'can't start new thread' directly.

questions.question_text: Give a repeatable 'works on my machine' Linux checklist you would run through when an ML wheel/container works on one host but crashes or gives wrong performance on another. Include at least: CPU ISA (AVX), glibc, NVIDIA driver/CUDA/toolkit, and kernel constraints. Provide concrete commands for each item.
questions.correct_answer:
Checklist + commands:

1) CPU instruction set / AVX support (common for TF/PyTorch/XGBoost wheels):
- `lscpu | egrep 'Model name|Flags'` (look for `avx`, `avx2`, `avx512*`)
- If illegal instruction occurs: confirm the wheel expects AVX2/AVX512.

2) glibc / distro baseline (manylinux compatibility, older servers):
- `ldd --version` (glibc version)
- `cat /etc/os-release`

3) libstdc++ / GCC runtime (GLIBCXX errors):
- `strings $(g++ -print-file-name=libstdc++.so.6) | grep GLIBCXX | tail`
- `ldd -v <python_extension.so> | grep -E 'libstdc\+\+|GLIBCXX'`

4) NVIDIA driver vs CUDA runtime compatibility:
- `nvidia-smi` (driver version, CUDA version reported by driver)
- `nvcc --version` (toolkit if installed)
- `python -c 'import torch; print(torch.version.cuda); print(torch.cuda.is_available())'`

5) CUDA/cuDNN/NCCL library resolution (container mounts, LD paths):
- `ldd -v <torch/_C*.so>` and/or `LD_DEBUG=libs python -c 'import torch'`

6) Kernel version / features (e.g., io_uring availability, cgroup v1/v2, overlayfs quirks, hugepages):
- `uname -r`
- `mount | head` (filesystem types)
- `stat -fc %T /` (fs type)

7) Container runtime differences (cgroups, seccomp, ulimits):
- `cat /proc/1/cgroup` and `mount | grep cgroup`
- `ulimit -a`

8) Performance-related environment differences:
- `env | egrep 'OMP|MKL|OPENBLAS|NUMEXPR|NCCL|CUDA|TORCH'`

The output guides whether the issue is ISA mismatch, ABI/runtime mismatch, driver/toolkit mismatch, or kernel/runtime constraints.
questions.explanation: Interviewers want a structured approach, not a one-off guess. These checks map directly to the most common ML portability failures: illegal instructions (AVX), missing/old glibc, `GLIBCXX` mismatches, CUDA driver/runtime incompatibilities, and kernel/cgroup constraints inside containers.

questions.question_text: A training job shows low GPU utilization and `top` shows Python at 300% CPU with very high `sy` (system CPU time). Which explanation is most plausible, and what is a good first diagnostic step?
options: A. The Python GIL is causing high system CPU; fix by using `asyncio`

B. The kernel is spending time in syscalls (e.g., heavy file I/O, page faults, networking, or many small reads); use `pidstat -w -p <pid>` or `strace -c -p <pid>` to see syscall/context switch patterns
C. High system CPU means the GPU driver is broken; reinstall CUDA
D. High system CPU always indicates too many Python threads; set `MKL_NUM_THREADS=1`
questions.explanation: System CPU time reflects time spent in kernel mode servicing syscalls and handling events like page faults, I/O, and scheduling. ML pipelines often hit this when the input pipeline does many small reads, decompresses via kernel-assisted paths, thrashes the page cache, or performs heavy networking. `pidstat -w` helps quantify context switches; `strace -c` summarizes syscall time/counts to quickly identify hotspots (e.g., `read`, `futex`, `poll`, `mmap`, `stat`).

questions.question_text: In a shared training environment, some users still cannot read checkpoints in `/checkpoints/run123` even though the directory has `drwxrws---` and group `ml`. One user needs read-only access without changing group membership. Propose a solution using ACLs and show the exact commands you would run.
questions.correct_answer:
Use POSIX ACLs to grant an individual user access.

Example (grant user `alice` read/execute on the directory and read on files):
1) Grant access on the directory (execute is needed to traverse):
`setfacl -m u:alice:rx /checkpoints/run123`
2) Grant default ACL so newly created files/dirs inherit permissions for alice:
`setfacl -d -m u:alice:rx /checkpoints/run123`
3) If existing files need read permission too:
`setfacl -R -m u:alice:rX /checkpoints/run123`
4) Verify:
`getfacl /checkpoints/run123 | sed -n '1,120p'`

This avoids changing group membership and keeps the base mode bits restrictive for others.
questions.explanation: Mode bits (user/group/other) are coarse. ACLs let you surgically grant access to a specific user or additional groups—common in shared ML clusters where datasets/checkpoints must be controlled but collaboration is needed. Default ACLs are important so new artifacts remain accessible.

questions.question_text: Your team shares a dataset directory `/data/datasets/projectA`. You want everyone in Unix group `ml` to be able to read/write new files, and you want new subdirectories to automatically inherit group `ml` (to avoid files being created with the creator's primary group). Which setup best matches this goal?
options: A. `chown -R root:root /data/datasets/projectA` and `chmod -R 777 /data/datasets/projectA`
B. `chgrp -R ml /data/datasets/projectA` then `chmod -R g+rwX /data/datasets/projectA` and set the setgid bit on directories: `find ... -type d -exec chmod g+s {} +`
C. Only set `umask 000` for all users
D. Use `sudo` for all writes to the dataset directory
questions.explanation: Group ownership plus group permissions control access in shared environments. Setting the setgid bit (`g+s`) on directories makes new files/dirs inherit the directory's group (here `ml`), preventing the 'wrong group' problem. `g+rwX` grants group read/write and execute on directories (and on files that already have execute). This is the standard pattern for shared ML datasets/checkpoints. `777` is overly permissive and risky; `umask` alone doesn't enforce group inheritance; `sudo` doesn't scale.

questions.question_text: In a shared GPU server, a teammate installs a custom `libcudnn.so` and your model import starts failing even though `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` points to your conda env. Explain how RPATH/RUNPATH and `/etc/ld.so.cache` can override expectations, and outline a practical troubleshooting sequence (commands + what you look for).
questions.correct_answer:
RPATH/RUNPATH are embedded search paths inside an ELF binary/extension module. The dynamic loader may prioritize these over parts of `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` depending on whether RPATH or RUNPATH is present and the loader's rules. Separately, `/etc/ld.so.cache` (built by `ldconfig`) caches system library locations from `/etc/ld.so.conf` and `/etc/ld.so.conf.d/*`, so a newly added system path can cause the loader to find a different `libcudnn.so` than expected.

Troubleshooting sequence:
1) Identify the failing extension and its dependencies: `python -c 'import torch; import os; print(torch._C.__file__)'` then `ldd -v <that .so>` to see which `libcudnn.so` / `libcublas.so` is chosen.
2) Inspect embedded paths: `readelf -d <.so> | egrep 'RPATH|RUNPATH'` (or `patchelf --print-rpath`). If an unexpected RPATH points to a system directory, it can force the wrong library.
3) Check loader resolution live: `LD_DEBUG=libs python -c 'import torch' 2>&1 | head -200` to see the exact search order and chosen paths.
4) Inspect cache/system config: `ldconfig -p | grep cudnn` and review `/etc/ld.so.conf.d/` entries to see if a system cudnn path is registered.
5) Fix by removing/adjusting RPATH (e.g., `patchelf --set-rpath`), ensuring consistent CUDA/cuDNN versions, or isolating with containers that control `/etc/ld.so.conf*` and mounted library paths.
questions.explanation: Many ML 'mystery import errors' come from the dynamic loader choosing a different `.so` than you think. `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is only one input; embedded RUNPATH/RPATH and the system cache can be decisive, especially when mixing conda, system CUDA installs, and container mounts.

questions.question_text:
You import a Python package (e.g., `import xgboost` or `import torch`) and get:

`ImportError: /.../libstdc++.so.6: version 'GLIBCXX_3.4.29' not found`

`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is already set to include your conda env. Which is the best next step to *confirm* what shared library is being loaded and from where?
options: A. Run `strace -f -e trace=network python -c 'import xgboost'`
B. Run `ldd -v $(python -c 'import xgboost,inspect,os; import xgboost.core; print(xgboost.core.__file__)')`
C. Run `top` and check the RES memory of the python process
D. Run `chmod +x libstdc++.so.6` inside the environment
questions.explanation: `GLIBCXX_*` errors are almost always `libstdc++` ABI/version mismatches. `ldd` (and `ldd -v`) on the specific extension module (.so) shows which `libstdc++.so.6` is resolved and which symbol versions are required/provided. This is the quickest way to confirm the actual resolution path before changing environment variables or reinstalling packages. (You can also use `readelf -d` / `objdump -p` to inspect RPATH/RUNPATH.)

questions.question_text: A training script uses `fork`-based multiprocessing to share a large in-memory dataset cache across DataLoader workers. After a few epochs, memory usage grows until the container OOMs. Explain, at the Linux process/memory level, how copy-on-write (CoW) can cause this and give two concrete mitigations.
questions.correct_answer:
With `fork`, child processes initially share the parent's memory pages via copy-on-write. The large dataset cache appears shared until either the parent or any worker writes to those pages (including seemingly minor mutations like updating a Python object, reference counts, or touching a NumPy array). When a page is written, the kernel copies that page for the writing process, so the 'shared' cache becomes duplicated across workers over time, causing RSS to grow and eventually OOM.

Mitigations: (1) Avoid in-place mutations of shared structures; make the cached data truly read-only (e.g., memory-map files, use immutable arrays, avoid Python object graphs). (2) Use `spawn` and load data per worker (possibly with local on-disk cache) instead of relying on CoW sharing; or use shared memory explicitly (e.g., `torch.Tensor.share_memory_()`, `multiprocessing.shared_memory`) to control what is shared. (3) Move caches to disk via mmap/LMDB and rely on page cache rather than per-process heap.
questions.explanation: CoW is page-granular and triggered by writes, not by reads. Python workloads often 'write' more than expected (object metadata, refcounts, allocator behavior), which silently breaks sharing. Understanding RSS growth patterns in `ps`, `smem`, or cgroup memory metrics is key to diagnosing this in ML data pipelines.

questions.question_text:
You have a PyTorch training job on Linux that uses DataLoader with num_workers>0. The job sometimes hangs at the first batch when run inside a container, but works on your laptop. Which change is the most reliable first fix and why?

```python
loader = DataLoader(ds, num_workers=8, pin_memory=True)
for batch in loader:
    ...
```
options: A. Set `torch.set_num_threads(1)` to avoid thread oversubscription
B. Set multiprocessing start method to `spawn` (or `forkserver`) and guard code with `if __name__ == "__main__":`
C. Export `OMP_NUM_THREADS=64` so workers finish faster
D. Disable `pin_memory` because it causes deadlocks on Linux
questions.explanation: In Linux the default multiprocessing start method is often `fork`, which can inherit a partially-initialized runtime (CUDA context, threadpools, OpenMP/MKL state, dataloader worker init) and lead to deadlocks/hangs that are environment-dependent (containers, different libc, different drivers). Using `spawn` (or `forkserver`) starts a fresh Python interpreter process, avoiding unsafe state inheritance. The `if __name__ == "__main__":` guard prevents recursive process spawning on `spawn` and is required for correctness.

questions.question_text:
On a dual-socket NUMA machine, your CPU utilization is high but dataloader throughput is inconsistent and GPU utilization oscillates. You suspect thread oversubscription and poor CPU locality.

What concrete Linux-level actions would you take to diagnose and improve CPU performance for the input pipeline (NUMA/affinity/worker placement/OpenMP/MKL)?
questions.correct_answer:
Diagnose:
- Check topology: `lscpu`, `numactl -H`.
- Observe CPU placement and migrations: `ps -eLo pid,tid,psr,comm | grep -E 'python|dataloader'`, `taskset -pc <pid>`, and `perf stat`/`perf top` if available.
- Check oversubscription: count threads per process; inspect env vars `OMP_NUM_THREADS`, `MKL_NUM_THREADS`, `OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS`, `NUMEXPR_NUM_THREADS`.
Improve:
- Pin processes/workers to cores/NUMA nodes: run training with `numactl --cpunodebind=<n> --membind=<n>` or use `taskset`/affinity APIs; ensure dataloader workers are placed on appropriate cores.
- Prevent thread-pool explosion: set `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` (or a small value) for dataloader-heavy workloads; similarly cap MKL/OpenBLAS threads; avoid `num_workers * OMP_NUM_THREADS` greatly exceeding physical cores.
- Align memory locality: bind memory allocations to the same NUMA node as the CPU doing decoding/preprocessing; consider separate binding for GPU-attached NUMA node.
- If using multiple GPUs, assign per-GPU worker pools pinned to the closest NUMA node.
- Validate by measuring steady-state throughput and reduced context switching/migrations (`pidstat -w`, `perf`).
questions.explanation: Input pipelines often combine multiprocessing (DataLoader workers) with native threaded libraries (JPEG decode, BLAS, tokenizers). On NUMA systems, poor affinity causes remote memory access and scheduler migrations; oversubscription causes context-switch overhead. Practical fixes involve binding CPU/memory locality and capping thread pools so total runnable threads match available cores.

questions.question_text:
You’re optimizing input pipeline performance. Dataset is millions of small JPEGs on NFS/EFS; GPU is underutilized. You can’t change the model, but you can change how data is stored/read.

Give two Linux/IO-informed changes you’d try (and why) to improve throughput. Your answer should mention at least one of: page cache, readahead, mmap vs read, small-file amplification, or local NVMe caching.
questions.correct_answer:
Two strong changes:
1) Reduce small-file amplification by packing images into larger contiguous files (e.g., tar shards, WebDataset, TFRecords/LMDB). NFS/EFS suffers from metadata latency (lots of `open/stat/close`), so fewer larger reads typically improves throughput and makes readahead/page cache more effective.
2) Introduce local caching on NVMe (or instance store): pre-stage shards to local disk or use a caching layer so repeated epochs hit local page cache/NVMe rather than remote network storage. This reduces network round trips and tail latency.
Additional IO-informed tweaks (acceptable variants): increase read parallelism carefully; tune readahead for sequential shard reads; prefer streaming reads over random small reads; consider `mmap` for large contiguous datasets (but be mindful of page-fault overhead and memory pressure); ensure enough RAM for page cache and avoid thrashing.
questions.explanation: The bottleneck is often filesystem metadata and network latency rather than raw bandwidth. Linux page cache and readahead work best with larger sequential reads. Packing data and using local NVMe caching are common real-world fixes to raise GPU utilization in training pipelines.

questions.question_text:
A high-QPS inference service starts failing with errors like “Too many open files” and clients see connection resets under load. Which set of Linux tunings/checks is MOST relevant to investigate first?

Assume the app uses HTTP keep-alive but still opens many sockets.
options: A. Only increase GPU power limit; file descriptors are unrelated to networking
B. Check and raise per-process `ulimit -n`, system `fs.file-max`, and network backlog settings like `net.core.somaxconn`; also verify the service’s listen backlog and connection handling
C. Disable TCP and use only UDP to avoid file descriptors
D. Increase swap size; it directly increases the number of file descriptors available
questions.explanation: Each socket consumes a file descriptor, so high connection counts can hit per-process (`RLIMIT_NOFILE`, `ulimit -n`) and system-wide (`fs.file-max`) limits. Under load, accept queues/backlogs can overflow if `somaxconn` and the application’s listen backlog are too low, leading to connection drops/resets. A practical investigation checks current limits, the number of open FDs (`ls /proc/<pid>/fd | wc -l`), socket states (`ss -s` / `ss -tan state time-wait`), and then tunes limits/backlogs alongside application-level connection reuse.

questions.question_text:
A model server in a container gets OOM-killed even though `free -h` on the host shows plenty of RAM. You suspect cgroups.

Explain how you’d confirm cgroup memory limits and pressure (memory.high vs memory.max), and name two practical remediation options for an ML inference service.
questions.correct_answer:
Confirm:
- Determine cgroup v2 path: `cat /proc/self/cgroup` (inside container) and check mount: `mount | grep cgroup2`.
- Inspect limits/usage: `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max`, `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.high`, `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.current`, and `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.events` (look for `oom`/`oom_kill`).
- Check kernel OOM signals: `dmesg -T | grep -i oom` (host) and container runtime events.
- Also check `pids.max` and `cpu.max` if symptoms include throttling.
Remediation (examples):
1) Increase container memory limit (`memory.max`) or set a less aggressive `memory.high` so the service isn’t constantly throttled/reclaimed.
2) Reduce memory footprint: lower batch size, enable model quantization, limit worker processes, avoid duplicating model weights across processes, use shared memory optimizations, or switch preload/fork strategy.
Optionally adjust OOM behavior: set `oom_score_adj` for critical processes (with care) and ensure swap settings align with latency goals.
questions.explanation: Containers are constrained by cgroups, so host free memory can be irrelevant. `memory.max` is a hard limit; `memory.high` triggers reclaim/throttling before the hard limit. ML services often duplicate memory across workers or load large models, making cgroup-aware diagnosis essential.

questions.question_text:
Your distributed training job occasionally hangs during all-reduce. It only happens on one cluster, and logs show intermittent socket connect failures. Which Linux/networking concept is MOST relevant if you see many short-lived TCP connections and a spike in `TIME_WAIT`?

Assume the training code opens many connections (or reconnects) during rendezvous/health checks.
options: A. DNS caching cannot affect TCP; ignore DNS and only check GPU utilization
B. Ephemeral port exhaustion and `TIME_WAIT` buildup can prevent new outbound connections; connection pooling/reuse and reducing reconnect storms are typical fixes
C. MTU only affects UDP, not TCP, so it can’t cause hangs
D. Routing tables are static; if it works once it can’t fail later
questions.explanation: If a host creates many short-lived outbound TCP connections, it can run out of ephemeral ports, especially when sockets linger in `TIME_WAIT`. This can manifest as connect failures or stalls, impacting distributed training coordination and RPC traffic. Practical mitigations include reusing connections (pooling/keep-alive), reducing reconnect loops, tuning client behavior, and in some cases adjusting OS TCP settings (carefully) and scaling out rendezvous services.

questions.question_text:
A teammate reports: “Model training is slower on machine B than machine A, but the code and requirements.txt are the same.” You suspect environment/driver differences.

What Linux-side information would you capture in a bug report to make ML performance/repro issues actionable (OS/kernel/driver/CUDA/library), and how would you capture it reproducibly?
questions.correct_answer:
Capture:
- OS + kernel: `cat /etc/os-release`, `uname -a`.
- CPU/memory/NUMA: `lscpu`, `numactl -H`, `free -h`.
- GPU + driver: `nvidia-smi -a` (driver version, GPU model, clocks), `modinfo nvidia | head`.
- CUDA toolkit/runtime: `nvcc --version` (if present), `python -c "import torch; print(torch.version.cuda, torch.cuda.is_available()); print(torch.__version__)"`.
- cuDNN/NCCL (if relevant): from framework diagnostics or package versions.
- Python env: `python -V`, `pip freeze` or `conda env export`, plus the exact command line used.
- Container details if applicable: image digest, `docker inspect`/`podman inspect`, or `/etc/ld.so.cache` relevant libs.
- Key env vars that affect perf: `OMP_NUM_THREADS`, `MKL_NUM_THREADS`, `NCCL_*`, `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`, `TOKENIZERS_PARALLELISM`.
- Storage and filesystem type for datasets: `df -T`, mount options.
Reproducible capture: commit a script (e.g., `collect_sysinfo.sh`) that prints all of the above, store it alongside experiment metadata, and attach outputs to the issue; optionally save as JSON for structured comparison across machines.
questions.explanation: ML reproducibility/performance often depends on kernel, drivers, CUDA/NCCL, CPU topology, and thread/runtime env vars—not just Python packages. Interviewers want to see you can produce a “minimum actionable” sysinfo bundle that lets others reproduce or at least explain the delta between machines.

questions.question_text:
Your box has 2 TB free, but training intermittently fails writing temporary files and dataloader errors mention `/tmp`. You also notice Docker is installed and the job runs in containers.

Describe a practical playbook to diagnose and mitigate disk/inode exhaustion for ML artifacts and container overlays. Include which directories you’d check and what fixes you’d apply without losing important checkpoints.
questions.correct_answer:
Playbook:
1) Identify what is actually full:
- `df -h` for block usage and `df -i` for inode usage, paying attention to separate mounts: `/`, `/var`, `/tmp`, container runtime dirs.
- `mount | column -t` to see if `/tmp` is tmpfs or a small partition.
2) Find top consumers on the affected filesystem (stay on same mount with `-xdev`):
- `sudo du -xh --max-depth=2 /var | sort -h | tail` (or `/`, `/tmp`, `/home`).
- For inode hotspots: `sudo find /path -xdev -type f | wc -l` by subdir; or `sudo du -x --inodes /path/* 2>/dev/null | sort -n | tail` (if supported).
3) ML-specific suspects:
- `~/.cache` (huggingface, torch extensions, pip), `~/.nv` caches, experiment tracking dirs, dataset extraction dirs.
- `/tmp` and `/dev/shm` usage from dataloaders, multiprocessing, and temp downloads.
- Docker overlay growth: `/var/lib/docker/overlay2` (or containerd under `/var/lib/containerd`). Check with `docker system df`.
4) Mitigations:
- Move caches to a larger volume: set `HF_HOME`, `TRANSFORMERS_CACHE`, `TORCH_HOME`, `XDG_CACHE_HOME`, and point temp to a large mount (`TMPDIR=/mnt/large/tmp`).
- Clean safe caches: remove old wheels/compile caches, prune old experiment runs; keep checkpoints by whitelisting checkpoint directories.
- For Docker: `docker system prune` (careful), prune unused images/volumes; enforce log rotation.
- Reduce small-file explosion: store artifacts as tar/shards, limit checkpoint frequency/retain last N, use `save_total_limit`, write fewer per-step files.
5) Prevent recurrence:
- Add monitoring/alerts on both blocks and inodes; document cache locations; ensure container writable layer isn’t the only storage for artifacts (mount a volume for checkpoints).
questions.explanation: This tests whether the candidate can connect ML artifact patterns (caches, checkpoints, extracted datasets) and container overlay growth to Linux filesystems and inodes. The key is distinguishing block vs inode exhaustion, checking the right mounts (`/tmp`, `/var/lib/docker`), and applying mitigations that preserve important model artifacts.

questions.question_text:
A training job crashes with:

- `OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device`
- but `df -h` shows plenty of free disk space.

On Linux, what is the MOST likely cause in ML workflows, and what command(s) confirm it?
options: A. The GPU ran out of VRAM; confirm with `nvidia-smi`
B. Inode exhaustion from many small files (checkpoints, shards, cache); confirm with `df -i` and locate hotspots with `find ... -xdev -printf`/`du --inodes`-style approaches
C. Kernel bug; confirm by upgrading the kernel
D. RAM exhaustion; confirm with `free -m`
questions.explanation: Disk space errors can come from exhausting inodes (file metadata entries) rather than data blocks. ML pipelines often create huge numbers of small files: dataset shards, extracted archives, tokenization caches, experiment artifacts, Docker overlay layers, etc. `df -i` shows inode usage. Remediation is deleting/compacting small files, moving caches, using tar/shards, cleaning `~/.cache`, `/tmp`, and Docker (`docker system prune`) where appropriate.

questions.question_text:
You deploy a model HTTP service on a Linux VM. Users report timeouts. You can SSH into the box. Which workflow is BEST to quickly distinguish: app not listening vs wrong port/bind vs firewall/security group vs upstream network issues?

Pick the best ordered set of checks.
options: A. Restart the VM, then check logs if it happens again

B. `curl -v http://127.0.0.1:PORT/health`; `ss -lntp | grep PORT`; `curl --connect-timeout 2 -w 'dns:%{time_namelookup} connect:%{time_connect} ttfb:%{time_starttransfer}\n' http://PUBLIC_DNS:PORT/health`; then check host firewall (`nft list ruleset`/`iptables -S`) and cloud security group/NACL
C. Only run `ping` to the public IP; if it responds the service is fine
