D. Only run `netstat -rn` to check routing; if routes exist the application must be listening
questions.explanation: A canonical troubleshooting flow is: (1) test locally via loopback to validate the app is up and responding, (2) confirm a listening socket and which process owns it (`ss -lntp`), (3) test from the host to the public name/IP with curl timing breakdown to separate DNS vs connect vs server response (TTFB), and (4) if local works but remote fails, inspect host firewall and cloud security controls. This distinguishes app-level failures (no listener, wrong bind address like 127.0.0.1) from OS-level filtering and network path issues.

questions.question_text:
You run a training script under systemd or Kubernetes. When you press Ctrl+C in an interactive terminal you get a clean shutdown, but when the orchestrator stops the job it sometimes hangs and leaves DataLoader workers behind.

Explain what happens on Ctrl+C vs orchestrator stop in terms of Linux signals (SIGINT/SIGTERM/SIGKILL), process groups, and what you would implement in the training code to ensure child processes are cleaned up reliably.
questions.correct_answer:
Ctrl+C sends SIGINT to the foreground process group of the controlling TTY (typically the main training process and any children in the same process group). Orchestrators usually send SIGTERM to the container’s PID 1 (or the main process) first, wait a grace period, then send SIGKILL if it doesn’t exit. If your training process spawns worker processes (DataLoader, distributed workers) that are not in the same process group/session or are not being reaped/terminated, they can become orphans (re-parented to PID 1) or remain running until killed. Zombies can appear if the parent exits without `wait()`-ing on children.

Mitigations: (1) Install signal handlers for SIGINT and SIGTERM that set a shutdown flag and trigger a controlled teardown (stop training loop, close dataloaders, join worker processes). (2) Ensure children are in the same process group so a group kill works (e.g., `os.setsid()` in parent then kill process group with `os.killpg`, or use `subprocess.Popen(..., start_new_session=True)` and manage group). (3) In Python multiprocessing, call `terminate()`/`kill()` and `join()` on workers; ensure the parent reaps children. (4) In containers, avoid having a shell as PID 1 that doesn’t forward signals; use a proper init (tini) or run the Python process as PID 1. (5) Handle SIGKILL cannot be caught; rely on SIGTERM grace period and frequent checkpoints to tolerate hard kills.
questions.explanation: Interviewers are probing whether you can connect ML training behavior (hanging shutdown, leftover workers) to Linux signal delivery and process lifecycle. Ctrl+C is SIGINT to the foreground process group; orchestrators use SIGTERM then SIGKILL. Reliable cleanup requires explicit signal handling, correct process-group management, and reaping/joining children to avoid zombies/orphans. For ML workloads, this prevents stuck jobs, leaked CPU, and corrupted checkpoints.

questions.question_text:
In Python multiprocessing on Linux, you can choose start methods: `fork`, `spawn`, or `forkserver`. For an ML training script that uses CUDA and a multi-worker DataLoader, which start method is generally safest and why?

(Assume PyTorch + CUDA; focus on Linux process semantics.)
options: A. `fork` is safest because copy-on-write makes CUDA contexts shareable across workers
B. `spawn` is generally safest because it starts a fresh interpreter and avoids inheriting unsafe state (e.g., CUDA runtime, thread pools) from the parent process
C. `forkserver` is unsafe because it disables copy-on-write and duplicates all memory immediately
D. All start methods are equivalent on Linux because the kernel normalizes them to the same syscalls
questions.explanation: With CUDA, forking a process after CUDA has been initialized can lead to subtle issues because the child inherits the parent’s address space and runtime state (threads, locks, CUDA context-related state). `spawn` launches a clean Python process and imports your module, avoiding inherited state and many deadlock/crash scenarios. `forkserver` can also be safer than `fork` in some cases, but the common guidance for CUDA-heavy workloads is to avoid `fork` unless you are certain CUDA is not initialized pre-fork. Copy-on-write helps memory efficiency but does not make complex runtime state (locks, threads, GPU contexts) safe to inherit.

questions.question_text:
You have a PyTorch training job where increasing `num_workers` in the DataLoader from 0 to 8 makes throughput worse and CPU usage spikes. On Linux, which explanation is MOST likely and ML-relevant?

Assume: dataset uses Python-heavy transforms, model runs on GPU, and the machine has 16 vCPUs.
options: A. Linux threads are always faster than processes; DataLoader uses threads so it must be a kernel scheduling bug

B. DataLoader uses multiple processes; Python-heavy transforms cause high per-process overhead and contention (e.g., serialization, page cache misses), and you may be oversubscribing CPU with BLAS/OpenMP threads too
C. Copy-on-write makes each worker share all memory forever, so throughput cannot decrease with more workers
D. The slowdown is because the GPU driver blocks forked children from running any CPU code
questions.explanation: PyTorch DataLoader typically uses multiprocessing (processes) rather than threads to bypass the Python GIL for Python-level work. More workers can reduce throughput when (1) CPU is oversubscribed (e.g., each worker + MKL/OpenMP threads multiplies runnable threads), (2) IPC/serialization and Python object overhead dominates, (3) storage/page-cache patterns worsen (random reads, many small files), or (4) workers contend on shared resources (filesystem, decompression libraries). Copy-on-write helps at fork time but doesn’t guarantee sustained sharing; once pages are written, they are duplicated. A practical fix is to cap thread pools (e.g., `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`, `MKL_NUM_THREADS=1`), tune `num_workers`, use `persistent_workers=True`, increase `prefetch_factor`, or move transforms to vectorized / C++ ops.

questions.question_text:
You deploy an ML inference API via systemd. The service works when run manually but fails under systemd because it can’t find the model path and also restarts in a tight loop after a transient failure. Write a minimal systemd unit snippet that:
- sets an environment variable `MODEL_DIR=/models/v1`
- sets a sensible restart policy with a backoff
- raises open file limit to 65536
- ensures logs are visible via `journalctl`

Provide the unit file content (only the relevant sections).
questions.correct_answer:
[Unit]
Description=ML Inference Service
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
Environment="MODEL_DIR=/models/v1"
# or: EnvironmentFile=/etc/myservice.env
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/inference-server --model-dir ${MODEL_DIR}
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5s
StartLimitIntervalSec=60
StartLimitBurst=5
LimitNOFILE=65536
# Logging to journald is default; explicitly:
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
questions.explanation: systemd does not inherit your interactive shell environment, so you must set env vars via `Environment=` or `EnvironmentFile=`. Restart behavior should avoid tight crash loops: `Restart=on-failure` plus `RestartSec` provides backoff; `StartLimit*` prevents infinite rapid restarts. `LimitNOFILE` sets RLIMIT_NOFILE for the service process, crucial for high-connection inference servers. Journald captures stdout/stderr by default; `journalctl -u <unit>` is the primary debugging tool.

questions.question_text:
A high-QPS inference service makes outbound HTTP calls to an upstream feature store. Under load, new connections fail with `cannot assign requested address`, and `ss -s` shows many sockets in `TIME-WAIT`. Which change is the best first fix at the application/Linux boundary?

Pick the best answer.
options: A. Increase the ephemeral port range and also implement connection pooling/keep-alives to reduce new connections and TIME_WAIT pressure.
B. Disable TCP entirely and switch to UDP to avoid TIME_WAIT.
C. Increase GPU memory; TIME_WAIT is a GPU networking limitation.
D. Set `ulimit -n` lower to force the app to use fewer sockets.
questions.explanation: The error typically indicates ephemeral port exhaustion due to too many short-lived outbound connections and TIME_WAIT accumulation. The most effective fix is usually at the app layer: reuse connections (HTTP keep-alive, pooling) to reduce churn. On the OS side, expanding `net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range` can help, and you should also ensure FD limits are sufficient. Simply lowering FD limits makes failures happen sooner; switching to UDP isn’t a drop-in replacement for HTTP/TCP.

questions.question_text:
Your training pipeline reads millions of small image files from a network filesystem and becomes IOPS-bound. After some time, operations like creating new cache files fail even though `df -h` shows plenty of free space. What is the most likely cause and a practical mitigation?

Pick the best answer.
options: A. You ran out of inodes; mitigate by checking `df -i` and reducing small-file count (e.g., tar/shard into larger files like WebDataset/TFRecords) or recreating filesystem with more inodes.
B. The filesystem block size is too large; mitigate by setting block size to 512 bytes at runtime.
C. Linux cannot handle more than 1 million files in a directory; mitigate by switching to Windows.
D. You hit the GPU’s file limit; mitigate by increasing VRAM.
questions.explanation: Filesystems allocate metadata structures called inodes; you can exhaust inodes long before disk space. Small-file ML datasets and caches are common inode killers. `df -i` reveals inode usage. Practical mitigations include sharding (tar/zip/WebDataset), using fewer files, hierarchical directory layouts, or provisioning storage with adequate inode density. This is a common real-world ML data pipeline issue, especially on shared or networked storage.

questions.question_text:
You need to improve CPU-side throughput for training on a dual-socket NUMA machine (2x CPU, 8x GPUs). Dataloader workers are CPU-heavy (JPEG decode) and you observe unstable step times. What Linux-level tuning approach is most appropriate?

Pick the best answer.
options: A. Increase `nice` to a higher value (more priority) for all processes so they run faster.

B. Pin the training process and its dataloader workers to specific CPU cores and align memory allocation with NUMA locality (e.g., `taskset`, `numactl --cpunodebind/--membind`), avoiding cross-socket traffic.
C. Disable hyperthreading in BIOS; it always improves ML throughput.
D. Set `ulimit -n` higher; it directly increases CPU performance.
questions.explanation: On NUMA systems, performance can suffer when threads bounce across sockets or allocate memory on a remote NUMA node. Binding CPU affinity and memory policy can stabilize and improve throughput, especially for CPU-heavy preprocessing feeding GPUs. `nice` changes scheduling priority (higher numeric nice = lower priority); it won’t fix NUMA locality. Disabling HT can help sometimes but is not the first targeted Linux tuning step. File descriptor limits are unrelated to CPU throughput.

questions.question_text:
A PyTorch training job uses `DataLoader(num_workers=8, pin_memory=True)` inside a Docker container. After a few minutes it crashes with an OOM, but `nvidia-smi` shows plenty of free GPU memory and `free -h` shows available memory due to cache. Which Linux memory concept is most likely involved, and what is a practical fix?

Pick the best answer.
options: A. The page cache is counted as RSS, so Linux is lying; disable page cache entirely.
B. `/dev/shm` (shared memory) is too small (common in containers); increase shared memory size (e.g., `--shm-size` or `--ipc=host`) and/or reduce workers/prefetch.
C. Overcommit is disabled, so `malloc` always fails immediately; enable overcommit and the problem disappears permanently.
D. Swap is required for GPU memory allocations; add swap to increase VRAM.
questions.explanation: PyTorch multiprocessing DataLoader often uses shared memory for moving batches between worker processes and the main process. In containers, `/dev/shm` is frequently limited (e.g., 64MB by default), causing OOM-like failures or crashes even when system RAM appears available. Increasing `/dev/shm` (Docker `--shm-size=...` or `--ipc=host` when appropriate) is a common fix, along with tuning `num_workers`, `prefetch_factor`, and batch size. Page cache vs RSS is real, but it doesn’t explain `/dev/shm` exhaustion; GPU memory is unrelated to `/dev/shm`.

questions.question_text:
An inference service starts throwing `OSError: [Errno 24] Too many open files` under load. You want an end-to-end understanding of limits. Explain:
1) soft vs hard `ulimit -n`
2) per-process vs system-wide limits
3) how systemd can override limits
4) how to verify the effective limit of a running process
questions.correct_answer:
1) Soft vs hard: the soft limit is the current enforced limit for the process; it can be raised up to the hard limit by the process/user (if permitted). The hard limit is the ceiling; raising it typically requires appropriate privileges or configuration.
2) Per-process vs system-wide: `ulimit -n` applies per process (and inherited by children). System-wide constraints also exist, e.g., `fs.file-max` (max open files system-wide) and per-user limits (PAM limits). You can hit either the per-process limit or overall system exhaustion.
3) systemd overrides: services started by systemd may not inherit your shell’s `ulimit`. Unit settings like `LimitNOFILE=` set RLIMIT_NOFILE for the service; defaults can also be set in `/etc/systemd/system.conf` and `/etc/systemd/user.conf`.
4) Verify effective limit: check `/proc/<pid>/limits` for the running process. Also confirm systemd’s view via `systemctl show <unit> -p LimitNOFILE` and inspect `cat /proc/sys/fs/file-max` plus current usage via `/proc/sys/fs/file-nr` or `lsof | wc -l` (with care).
questions.explanation: AI/ML services often open many sockets (client connections, upstream calls) and files (models, shards, logs). The key model is: RLIMITs are per-process and inherited; system-wide max and per-user policies can still cap you; systemd commonly surprises engineers because it sets limits at service start. `/proc/<pid>/limits` is the ground truth for the running process.

questions.question_text:
Your distributed training spans multiple nodes. Metrics and logs show events “out of order” across machines, and gradient all-reduce timeouts occur sporadically. What is the best Linux-focused remediation plan regarding time handling?

Assume you control the OS images.
questions.correct_answer: Standardize on UTC everywhere (servers, containers, logs) and ensure reliable time sync using NTP/chrony. Concretely: (1) set timezone to UTC (`timedatectl set-timezone UTC`) and log in UTC; (2) run and monitor `chronyd` (or another NTP client) on every node; verify sync via `chronyc tracking` / `timedatectl status`; (3) alert on clock offset/drift; (4) avoid manual time changes; if stepping is needed, understand that abrupt time jumps can break TLS, token expiry, and log correlation—prefer chrony’s slewing in steady state; (5) ensure containers inherit correct time settings (host time namespace) and that time sync is not blocked by firewall rules to NTP sources.
questions.explanation: Distributed training and service debugging depend on consistent timestamps. Clock skew makes cross-node log correlation misleading and can cause timeouts/lease expirations. UTC avoids timezone/DST ambiguity. Chrony/NTP keeps clocks aligned; `chronyc tracking` shows offset, frequency, and sync status. Understanding step vs slew matters: stepping can make time go backwards/forwards abruptly, confusing systems that assume monotonic progress; slewing gradually corrects drift.

questions.question_text:
You’re debugging why a containerized PyTorch job sees fewer CPUs and less memory than the host. You suspect cgroup limits. Which locations are the most relevant to inspect for runtime system state vs configuration?

Choose the best mapping.
options: A. `/etc` = live kernel state; `/proc` = static config files; `/var/log` = device drivers; `/sys` = user home directories
B. `/etc` = system configuration; `/var/log` = logs; `/proc` = per-process and kernel runtime info (incl. cgroups/limits exposure); `/sys` = kernel device/model attributes (sysfs)
C. `/etc` = logs; `/var/log` = configuration; `/proc` = only process lists; `/sys` = swap settings
D. `/proc` and `/sys` are both on-disk persistent filesystems, so they’re best for storing configs that survive reboot
questions.explanation: For production debugging, it’s critical to know the layout: `/etc` holds configuration (e.g., systemd units, daemon configs), `/var/log` holds logs, `/proc` is a virtual filesystem exposing runtime kernel/process info (e.g., `/proc/<pid>/status`, `/proc/meminfo`, often cgroup-related views), and `/sys` (sysfs) exposes device and kernel subsystem attributes (e.g., `/sys/devices`, `/sys/class/net`, NUMA nodes). `/proc` and `/sys` are not persistent storage; they reflect current kernel state.

questions.question_text:
A GPU inference service suddenly starts failing after a driver update. You suspect kernel/driver issues. Which `dmesg` pattern is MOST indicative of a GPU driver problem that could impact CUDA workloads?

Pick the best answer.
options: A. Repeated lines like: `EXT4-fs (nvme0n1p2): mounted filesystem with ordered data mode`
B. Lines like: `NVRM: Xid (PCI:0000:65:00): 79, GPU has fallen off the bus` or `amdgpu: GPU reset`
C. Lines like: `systemd[1]: Started User Manager for UID 1000`
D. Lines like: `sshd[1234]: Accepted publickey for ubuntu from ...`
questions.explanation: `dmesg` is the kernel ring buffer; it’s where the kernel and drivers log hardware/driver events. GPU driver errors often show as NVIDIA Xid messages, “GPU has fallen off the bus”, PCIe AER errors, GPU resets, or IOMMU faults. These correlate strongly with CUDA failures, device disappearing, or intermittent crashes. The other options are normal boot/mount/service/auth logs and usually not root causes of GPU compute failures.

questions.question_text:
You’re on-call because a GPU training job is “slow”. On the host, `uptime` shows `load average: 32.0, 31.5, 30.8` and `top` shows CPU utilization around ~15% on a 32 vCPU machine. Which interpretation is MOST accurate and actionable?

Assume the model code itself did not change.
options: A. The CPUs are 100% saturated; load average directly equals CPU utilization, so you need more vCPUs.
B. The system likely has many runnable or uninterruptible (I/O-wait) tasks; low CPU% suggests the load is dominated by blocking (e.g., disk/NFS, GPU driver waits), not compute saturation.
C. Load average only counts CPU-bound processes; low CPU% means the load average is incorrect and can be ignored.
D. Load average is only meaningful on single-core systems; on multi-core systems it should always be near 0.
questions.explanation: Load average is the average number of tasks that are either runnable (on the run queue) or stuck in uninterruptible sleep (often I/O wait, shown as state 'D'). It is not the same as CPU utilization. A high load average with low CPU% often means threads are blocked (e.g., waiting on storage, network filesystem, GPU driver calls, page faults, or contention). Actionable next steps include checking `vmstat 1` (runnable `r`, blocked `b`), `iostat -x 1`, `pidstat -w/-d`, and looking for D-state tasks in `ps -eo pid,stat,comm,wchan:32 | head`.

questions.question_text: Distributed training with NCCL works on one cluster but hangs on another. You suspect it’s using the wrong network interface and/or blocked ports. Name two concrete checks and two common environment variables you would use to triage NCCL networking issues on Linux.
questions.correct_answer:
Checks (examples):
- Verify which interfaces/IPs are present and routable: `ip addr`, `ip route`, and confirm nodes can reach each other on the expected subnet.
- Validate connectivity/ports between nodes (and that firewalls/security groups allow it): e.g., `nc -vz <peer> <port>` / `ss -lntp` on listeners, or use the cluster’s documented port ranges for your launcher.
- Inspect NCCL logs to see chosen interface and transport: set `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` and look for interface selection.

Common env vars (examples):
- `NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0` (or a pattern like `^lo,docker0` to exclude) to force interface selection.
- `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` (or `TRACE`) to increase verbosity.
Other frequently used ones include `NCCL_IB_DISABLE=1` (to disable InfiniBand if misconfigured) and `NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL` depending on hardware.
questions.explanation: NCCL hangs are often caused by (1) selecting a non-routable interface (docker bridge, loopback, wrong NIC/VLAN) or (2) blocked connectivity between nodes (firewalls/security groups, missing routes). Linux tools (`ip`, `ss`, `nc`) confirm interface/routing and port reachability. NCCL environment variables help force interface choice and emit logs that reveal where it’s stuck (e.g., bootstrap, socket connect, IB transport).

questions.question_text: A Kubernetes training job is repeatedly OOM-killed. The node has plenty of free RAM, but the container restarts and you see exit code 137 / `OOMKilled: true`. Which statement best explains what’s happening and what you should check first?
options: A. The host is out of memory; container limits don’t matter, so add more node RAM
B. The process exceeded the container’s cgroup memory limit; check the Pod’s memory requests/limits and the framework’s memory usage (and whether swap is disabled)
C. The GPU ran out of VRAM; Linux reports GPU OOM as exit code 137
D. The container was killed due to too many open files; Kubernetes reports that as OOMKilled
questions.explanation: In Kubernetes, memory limits are enforced via cgroups. A container can be OOM-killed when it exceeds its cgroup memory limit even if the host has free RAM. Exit code 137 often indicates SIGKILL, commonly used by the OOM killer. The first checks are the Pod’s `resources.requests/limits`, actual memory usage, and whether swap is disabled (common in k8s), which makes memory pressure more abrupt. GPU VRAM OOM typically surfaces as framework errors, not `OOMKilled: true`.

questions.question_text: A FastAPI model server works locally but is unreachable from other machines when deployed to a Linux VM. The app logs show it is listening on `127.0.0.1:8000`. What change is needed to make it reachable externally (assuming firewall/security groups allow it)?
options: A. Bind to `0.0.0.0` (e.g., `uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000`) so it listens on all interfaces
B. Bind to `localhost` to allow external access; `localhost` is a special public interface
C. Change the client to use `127.0.0.1` instead of the VM’s IP address
D. Add a route with `ip route add 0.0.0.0/0 dev lo`
questions.explanation: Binding to `127.0.0.1` (loopback) only accepts connections from the same machine. Binding to `0.0.0.0` listens on all network interfaces, allowing external clients to connect via the VM’s IP (subject to firewall/security group rules). Changing the client to use 127.0.0.1 would only work if the client is on the same host.

questions.question_text: You activate a conda environment but `python` and `pip` still point to the system installation, causing packages to install in the wrong place. Which of the following is the most likely root cause and fix?
options: A. Conda environments cannot manage `pip`; you must uninstall pip from conda

B. Your shell startup files are not initializing conda (e.g., missing `conda init`), so PATH isn’t updated; ensure conda activation modifies PATH and use `python -m pip` to target the active interpreter
C. Linux caches the `python` binary path forever; reboot to clear it
D. `pip` always installs globally unless run with `sudo`
questions.explanation: If conda isn’t properly initialized for the shell, activation may not correctly prepend the environment’s `bin/` to `PATH`, so `python`/`pip` resolve to system binaries. Fix by correctly setting up conda shell integration (`conda init` or sourcing the appropriate script) and verify with `which python`/`which pip`. Using `python -m pip ...` ensures you install into the currently selected interpreter, reducing PATH-related mistakes. Reboots and `sudo` are not the core issue.

questions.question_text:
You are writing a Bash script that orchestrates training and uploads artifacts. You want it to be robust: fail on errors, fail on unset variables, propagate pipeline failures, clean up temp files on exit, and retry a flaky command.

Write a minimal pattern (not a full script) showing:
- `set -euo pipefail`
- a `trap` for cleanup
- a `retry` function used to run `aws s3 cp ...` (or similar)

(Use pseudocode where needed.)
questions.correct_answer:
Example pattern:

```bash
set -euo pipefail

TMPDIR="$(mktemp -d)"
cleanup() {
  rm -rf "${TMPDIR}"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

retry() {
  local -r max_attempts="${1}"; shift
  local attempt=1
  local delay=1
  until "$@"; do
    if (( attempt >= max_attempts )); then
      return 1
    fi
    sleep "${delay}"
    attempt=$((attempt+1))
    delay=$((delay*2))
  done
}

# Example usage (quotes prevent word-splitting/globbing issues)
retry 5 aws s3 cp "${TMPDIR}/model.pt" "s3://my-bucket/models/model.pt"
```
questions.explanation: `set -euo pipefail` catches common failure modes in pipelines: `-e` exits on errors, `-u` errors on unset variables, and `pipefail` ensures a failure in any pipeline stage fails the pipeline. `trap ... EXIT` ensures cleanup runs on success or failure. A retry wrapper with exponential backoff is a common ML-pipeline pattern for flaky network/storage operations. Quoting variables prevents accidental word splitting and glob expansion.

questions.question_text: An ML pipeline uses cron to run a daily batch job at `02:30`. Twice a year the job either runs twice or not at all, and log correlation across services is confusing. What is the best practice to avoid these time-related issues on Linux systems?
options: A. Set all servers to local time and rely on NTP; DST issues only affect human-readable timestamps
B. Standardize on UTC for servers and logs, and schedule jobs in UTC (or use systemd timers with explicit timezone handling); include timezone/offset in timestamps
C. Disable NTP so the clock never jumps; cron is more stable without time synchronization
D. Set `LC_ALL=C` system-wide; locale settings prevent DST changes
questions.explanation: DST transitions can cause local times like 02:30 to be ambiguous or nonexistent. Standard practice is to run servers in UTC, emit UTC timestamps (or include an explicit offset), and schedule batch jobs in UTC. Systemd timers can be easier to reason about and can be configured with calendar expressions; either way, UTC reduces ambiguity and improves cross-system log correlation.

questions.question_text: Your inference service makes many short-lived outbound HTTP calls (e.g., to feature stores). During traffic spikes you see `connect: cannot assign requested address` and metrics show lots of sockets in `TIME_WAIT`. What Linux concept is most directly involved and what is a practical mitigation?
questions.correct_answer: This is commonly ephemeral port exhaustion on the client side (outbound connections consume a local ephemeral port until TIME_WAIT expires). Practical mitigations include: reusing connections (HTTP keep-alive / connection pooling), reducing connection churn, scaling out clients across more source IPs, and in some cases tuning kernel settings like `net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range` (wider range) and TIME_WAIT-related behavior (carefully).
questions.explanation: Each outbound TCP connection uses a local (source) ephemeral port. If the service creates connections faster than ports are released (TIME_WAIT), it can run out and fail to connect. The most robust fix is application-level connection reuse (pooling/keep-alive). Kernel tuning can help but should be done carefully and validated, as it affects the whole host.

questions.question_text: A model inference service on Linux starts failing under load with errors like `Too many open files` and sporadic connection failures. The service runs under systemd. Which approach best addresses the file descriptor limit in a production-safe way?
options: A. Run `ulimit -n 100000` in your interactive shell before starting the service; systemd will inherit it
B. Set `LimitNOFILE=100000` in the systemd unit (or drop-in), and verify the process limit via `/proc/<pid>/limits`
C. Export `NOFILE=100000` as an environment variable; Linux will read it as the fd limit
D. Increase only `/proc/sys/fs/file-max`; per-process limits are irrelevant
questions.explanation: Systemd services do not reliably inherit interactive shell `ulimit` settings. The correct place is the systemd unit (`LimitNOFILE=`) so the limit is applied consistently at service start. `/proc/<pid>/limits` confirms the effective limits. Raising `fs.file-max` only increases the system-wide ceiling; you still need per-process limits to prevent `EMFILE` errors.

questions.question_text: You are debugging a stuck training job in Kubernetes. The Pod is running but not making progress. You want to (1) view logs from the previous crashed container instance and (2) get an interactive shell inside the currently running container. What commands would you use (assume namespace `ml` and pod `trainer-abc` and container `worker`)?
questions.correct_answer:
1) Previous container logs: `kubectl -n ml logs trainer-abc -c worker --previous`
2) Exec into running container: `kubectl -n ml exec -it trainer-abc -c worker -- /bin/bash` (or `/bin/sh` if bash isn’t present)
questions.explanation: `kubectl logs --previous` is essential when the container has restarted and you need the logs from the prior instance. `kubectl exec -it ... -- <shell>` opens an interactive session inside the running container to inspect files, environment variables, GPU visibility, mounted volumes, etc. These are common operational basics ML engineers need when jobs stall or crash.

questions.question_text: A containerized PyTorch job inside Kubernetes sees `torch.cuda.is_available() == False` even though the node has GPUs. `nvidia-smi` inside the container fails with a driver-related message. Which statement best describes the CUDA driver/runtime compatibility model relevant here?
options: A. The CUDA runtime inside the container must exactly match the host driver version, otherwise GPUs are never usable
B. The host provides the NVIDIA kernel driver; the container can have a CUDA runtime/toolkit, but it must be compatible with the host driver (driver must be new enough for the runtime)
C. Installing CUDA toolkit in the container automatically installs the correct kernel driver on the host via DKMS
D. Kubernetes automatically emulates GPUs if the container runtime cannot mount the driver libraries
questions.explanation: In typical setups (NVIDIA Container Toolkit), the host supplies the kernel-mode driver and device files; the container provides user-space libraries (CUDA runtime, cuDNN, etc.). Compatibility generally requires the host driver to be sufficiently new to support the CUDA runtime version in the container. Exact matches are not required, but incompatible combinations lead to failures like missing libraries or driver/runtime mismatch errors. Containers do not install kernel drivers on the host, and Kubernetes does not emulate GPUs.

questions.question_text:
You build a Python wheel for a custom CUDA extension on Ubuntu 22.04 and it installs fine there, but on an older production image (e.g., CentOS 7) importing the package fails with an error like:

`ImportError: /lib64/libc.so.6: version 'GLIBC_2.28' not found`

What is the most appropriate fix for distributing this to heterogeneous Linux environments?
options: A. Statically link glibc into the wheel so it runs on any Linux distribution
B. Build wheels in a manylinux-compatible environment (or use auditwheel) targeting an older glibc baseline, and publish separate wheels if needed
C. Add `LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64` in the user’s shell startup files to force the right glibc at runtime
D. Pin Python to the same minor version across all machines; glibc issues are caused by Python version mismatches
questions.explanation: The error indicates the binary was compiled against a newer glibc than exists on the target system. The standard solution is to build wheels using a manylinux baseline (e.g., manylinux2014) so the wheel only requires an older glibc ABI. Tools like `auditwheel` help repair/validate wheels. You generally cannot safely statically link glibc (and it’s discouraged/unsupported in many contexts), and `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` hacks won’t conjure a compatible glibc. Python version pinning doesn’t address glibc ABI requirements.

questions.question_text: A CPU-heavy feature engineering pipeline uses Python multiprocessing and NumPy. On a dual-socket NUMA machine, performance is worse than on a smaller single-socket machine. Which Linux-level technique is most likely to help diagnose and improve this?
options: A. Set `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` and ignore NUMA; NUMA never affects Python workloads
B. Use `numactl`/CPU affinity (`taskset`) to pin processes/threads and memory allocation to the same NUMA node; inspect with `lscpu` and `numactl --hardware`
C. Increase the size of `/tmp` to speed up CPU operations
D. Disable the page cache to reduce memory usage
questions.explanation: On multi-socket systems, remote memory access can significantly slow down data pipelines. Pinning worker processes and their memory allocations to the same NUMA node can reduce cross-socket traffic. `lscpu` shows topology; `numactl` can control CPU/memory placement; `taskset` can pin CPU affinity. Threading libraries (OpenMP/MKL) can also oversubscribe cores, so affinity and thread settings often work together.

questions.question_text: You’re optimizing dataset loading. On one machine the dataset is on local NVMe; on another it’s on network storage (NFS/object store mounted via FUSE). Training throughput drops and GPU utilization is low. What Linux-level observations and mitigations are most appropriate?
questions.correct_answer: Observe: measure read throughput/IOPS and latency (e.g., `iostat -x`, `pidstat -d`, `sar -d`, `cat /proc/diskstats`), check if the loader is CPU-bound vs IO-bound (e.g., `top`, `pidstat -u`, `perf` if allowed), and inspect page cache behavior (e.g., `free -h`, `/proc/meminfo` for Cached, Dirty, Writeback). On network/FUSE, expect higher latency and different caching semantics; small random reads can be especially slow. Mitigate: increase sequentiality (tar/record/shards), use larger read sizes, prefetch and cache locally (e.g., copy to local SSD, use a local cache directory), tune DataLoader workers/prefetch, and consider `readahead`/mount options where appropriate. Validate by comparing GPU utilization and step time before/after.
questions.explanation: ML input pipelines are often limited by storage latency and IOPS rather than raw throughput, especially with many small files on network storage. Linux tools can reveal whether the bottleneck is disk, network filesystem overhead, or CPU decompression. Effective mitigations typically restructure data access patterns (sharding/streaming), leverage local SSD caching, and tune prefetch/parallelism to keep GPUs fed without overwhelming the storage backend.

questions.question_text: A training job running for days intermittently crashes and you want actionable diagnostics. Which Linux settings/approaches are most relevant for reliability and post-mortem debugging?
options: A. Increase `ulimit -n` (open files) and `ulimit -u` (max user processes/threads) as needed; enable core dumps (`ulimit -c`, `core_pattern`) and ensure disks have space to store them
B. Only increase swap; core dumps are not useful for native crashes in ML stacks
C. Disable limits entirely by running as root; this prevents failures
D. Set `PYTHONHASHSEED=random` to improve crash diagnostics
questions.explanation: Long-running ML workloads hit resource limits (FDs, processes/threads, locked memory) and can crash in native code (CUDA, MKL, custom ops). Raising limits appropriately prevents avoidable failures. Core dumps plus correct `core_pattern`/storage allow debugging segmentation faults and native crashes (often critical in ML). Running as root is not a safe fix; swap alone doesn’t address FD/process exhaustion.

questions.question_text: You need to deploy an inference service that pulls model weights from a private repo and also calls a paid API. Which practice best reflects good Linux security hygiene for handling secrets on the host?
options: A. Store API keys in the source repo’s README so the team can easily find them
B. Put secrets in `/tmp/secrets.txt` because `/tmp` is cleared on reboot

C. Use least-privilege: store secrets in root-owned files with restrictive permissions (e.g., `chmod 600`), prefer a secret manager or injected environment at runtime, and restrict SSH keys with correct permissions and passphrases
D. Add the private SSH key to the Docker image so every container has access
questions.explanation: ML engineers often need to handle credentials for model registries, artifact stores, and external APIs. Best practice is least privilege and minimizing exposure: restrictive file permissions, avoiding baking secrets into images, and using runtime injection/secret managers. SSH keys should have proper permissions (typically 600) and be scoped/rotated; embedding keys in images or repos is a major security risk.

questions.question_text: A model server listens on port 8080 inside a VM. From your laptop you get `connection refused`. On the VM, `curl localhost:8080` works. What sequence of Linux/network checks would you do to isolate whether it’s (a) the process binding, (b) firewall rules, (c) routing/security group, or (d) DNS? Provide concrete commands.
questions.correct_answer: Confirm binding: `ss -ltnp | grep 8080` to see if it’s listening on `127.0.0.1:8080` vs `0.0.0.0:8080`/VM IP. If it’s bound to loopback only, reconfigure the server to bind to the VM interface. Check firewall: `sudo iptables -S` or `sudo nft list ruleset`, and `sudo ufw status` (if used) to ensure inbound 8080 is allowed. Validate reachability from outside: from laptop `nc -vz <vm-ip> 8080` or `curl -v http://<vm-ip>:8080`. Check routing/security group/cloud ACLs: ensure the VM’s network security rules allow inbound 8080 and that you’re targeting the correct public/private IP. Check DNS: `dig <hostname>`/`getent hosts <hostname>` to confirm it resolves to the expected IP. Optionally capture packets: `sudo tcpdump -ni any port 8080` on the VM to see if SYNs arrive.
questions.explanation: This isolates common real-world ML serving issues: binding to localhost (common in dev), host firewall drops, upstream network policy/security groups, and DNS pointing to the wrong IP. `ss` shows the truth about where the process is listening. Firewall tools confirm local policy. `tcpdump` is definitive for whether traffic reaches the machine.

questions.question_text: In a distributed training job across multiple nodes, you see inconsistent event ordering and strange metric timestamps. Logs show some machines are ~90 seconds ahead. Which steps are most appropriate to debug and fix time synchronization on Linux?
options: A. Set `TZ=UTC` in the environment; this fixes clock drift between machines
B. Check local timezone only (`/etc/timezone`) and ignore NTP because it’s unrelated to distributed systems
C. Verify NTP/chrony status and offset (e.g., `timedatectl`, `chronyc tracking` or `ntpq -p`), confirm time sources, and standardize on UTC for logging/metrics
D. Disable time sync to prevent sudden clock changes; rely on application-level timestamps only
questions.explanation: Timezone affects representation, not the underlying clock drift. Distributed ML systems are sensitive to clock skew (ordering, TTLs, auth tokens, metric alignment). You should inspect time sync status, offsets, and sources using `timedatectl` plus chrony/ntpd tools, fix NTP configuration/firewall, and prefer UTC in logs/metrics for consistency. Disabling sync generally makes drift worse over time.

questions.question_text: A long-running inference process starts failing with `Too many open files`. You want to confirm whether it’s a file descriptor leak and what the process is holding. Which Linux paths/commands are most directly useful?
options: A. `cat /proc/<pid>/cmdline` and `echo $PATH`
B. `ls -l /proc/<pid>/fd` (or `lsof -p <pid>`) and `cat /proc/<pid>/limits` to see the open-file limit
C. `cat /proc/cpuinfo` and `uname -a`
D. `df -h` and `du -sh /var/log`
questions.explanation: `/proc/<pid>/fd` shows each open file descriptor and what it points to (sockets, files, pipes). `lsof -p` is a convenient view if available. `/proc/<pid>/limits` shows the current `Max open files` soft/hard limits, helping you distinguish “leak” from “limit too low.” The other options don’t reveal FD usage.

questions.question_text: You suspect a memory leak in a Python training process on Linux. Without installing extra tools, how can you quickly check (1) the process’s current RSS, (2) whether it’s growing over time, and (3) whether it’s hitting cgroup/container memory limits? Mention specific `/proc` paths/commands.
questions.correct_answer: 1) Current RSS: read `/proc/<pid>/status` (fields like `VmRSS`, `VmSize`) or `/proc/<pid>/statm`. 2) Growth over time: sample those values periodically (e.g., `watch -n1 'grep -E "VmRSS|VmSize" /proc/<pid>/status'` or a small loop) and correlate with timestamps. 3) Cgroup/container limits: check `/proc/<pid>/cgroup` to find the cgroup path, then read memory limit/usage from cgroup files (v2: `/sys/fs/cgroup/<path>/memory.max` and `memory.current`; v1: `memory.limit_in_bytes` and `memory.usage_in_bytes`). Also inspect `/proc/<pid>/limits` and dmesg/oom logs if OOM kills occur.
questions.explanation: `/proc` provides per-process kernel-reported metrics without extra packages. `VmRSS` is the resident set size (physical memory in use). Sampling over time shows trends indicative of leaks. In containers, the relevant memory ceiling is often the cgroup limit, not the host RAM; checking the process’s cgroup membership and reading the corresponding cgroup memory files explains why a process OOMs “early.”

questions.question_text: Your inference service writes temporary model artifacts during startup and sometimes crashes after a node reboot because the directory is missing. Where should temporary files go, and what should you assume about their lifetime on Linux?
options: A. `/tmp`; assume it may be cleared on reboot or by tmp cleaners, so don’t rely on persistence
B. `/var/log`; assume it is always writable and persistent across reboots
C. `/proc`; assume it is a stable on-disk filesystem suited for temporary data
D. `/sys`; assume it persists and is intended for application caches
questions.explanation: `/tmp` is the conventional location for temporary files. Many systems clean `/tmp` on reboot or via periodic cleanup, so applications must tolerate it being empty and should recreate needed directories/files. `/var/log` is for logs, `/proc` and `/sys` are virtual filesystems exposing kernel/process state and are not for storing application data.

questions.question_text: On a RHEL/CentOS-like system, your training job fails at runtime with `error while loading shared libraries: libgomp.so.1: cannot open shared object file`. You want a minimal, reproducible fix using yum/dnf. What would you do and why?
questions.correct_answer: Install the missing runtime library via the OS package manager (e.g., `dnf/yum install libgomp` or the package providing it), and pin/record the exact RPM versions (or use a locked repo/snapshot) so the environment is reproducible. Verify with `ldd <binary>` or `ldconfig -p | grep libgomp` and rerun. Avoid copying random .so files into the image; it’s brittle and can violate ABI expectations.
questions.explanation: AI/ML stacks often depend on OpenMP (libgomp) via numpy/scikit-learn/LightGBM/XGBoost or custom C++ extensions. The correct fix is to install the proper OS package that owns the library, ensuring updates/security and correct dependencies. For reproducibility, you should pin versions (or use repo snapshotting) and capture them in build artifacts (Dockerfile, lockfile, or image digest). `ldd` helps confirm which library is missing and from where it should be resolved.

questions.question_text: You need to build a Docker image for GPU training on Ubuntu where `apt-get install` sometimes pulls newer dependencies and breaks reproducibility. Which approach best improves reproducibility while still using apt in a realistic CI/CD setup?
options: A. Run `apt-get update` only once locally and commit `/var/lib/apt/lists` into git
B. Pin package versions (and ideally the base image digest), use `--no-install-recommends`, and snapshot the apt repository (or use a dated snapshot mirror)
C. Use `apt-get upgrade -y` during every build to ensure the latest security patches and consistent results
D. Replace apt with `pip install -U` for system libraries like `libstdc++` and `libssl`
questions.explanation: Reproducible builds require controlling inputs: the base image (use an immutable digest), the repository state (use snapshot/dated mirrors or an internal mirror), and the exact package versions. `--no-install-recommends` reduces variability by avoiding extra packages that can change over time. Committing apt lists isn’t a standard or reliable approach, upgrading each build breaks determinism, and pip cannot replace system package management for core OS libraries.

questions.question_text:
You’re optimizing CPU-side preprocessing for training on a dual-socket NUMA machine (2 NUMA nodes). GPU utilization is low because the input pipeline is inconsistent. Provide an end-to-end Linux recipe to improve throughput using NUMA awareness and CPU affinity.

Your answer should mention: how to observe NUMA topology, how to bind processes/threads/memory, and how to validate improvements.
questions.correct_answer: Recipe: (1) Observe topology: use `lscpu` (NUMA nodes, cores per socket), `numactl --hardware`, and optionally `hwloc-ls` to understand CPU/NUMA layout. (2) Bind CPU and memory: run the dataloader/preprocess workers pinned to a NUMA node close to the target GPU/PCIe root if possible. Use `numactl --cpunodebind=<n> --membind=<n> <command>` or `--physcpubind` for specific cores. For services, set affinity via `taskset` or systemd `CPUAffinity=`; for multi-threaded libs, control thread counts/affinity with `OMP_NUM_THREADS`, `MKL_NUM_THREADS`, and `KMP_AFFINITY`/`GOMP_CPU_AFFINITY`. (3) Avoid cross-node memory: ensure memory allocation happens after binding (so pages are allocated on the intended node); consider `numactl --interleave=all` only if access is uniform. (4) Validate: monitor throughput and latency; check `numastat -p <pid>` for remote vs local memory, `perf stat` for stalls, and observe GPU utilization. Compare end-to-end step time, dataloader time, and CPU run queue (`top`/`htop`) before/after. Iterate on worker count and core pinning to reduce contention.
questions.explanation: NUMA issues show up as “mysterious” input pipeline jitter. Binding CPU and memory can reduce remote memory accesses and cache misses, improving preprocessing consistency. The key is an end-to-end loop: inspect topology, bind appropriately, then validate with NUMA and performance metrics rather than guessing.

questions.question_text:
You’re using PyTorch `DataLoader(num_workers>0)` and `multiprocessing` on Linux. Sometimes the job hangs or uses far more memory than expected after you add a large global object (e.g., a tokenizer, a dataset index, or a huge cache) initialized in the parent process.

Explain how Linux process start methods (`fork` vs `spawn`) affect memory behavior and correctness, and what you would change in practice.
questions.correct_answer: On Linux, `fork` starts child processes by duplicating the parent process memory space using copy-on-write (COW). This can be memory-efficient if the parent’s memory is mostly read-only, but if workers mutate shared pages (or libraries allocate after fork), it can trigger large memory duplication. Forking after initializing large Python objects can also cause subtle correctness issues with threads, locks, CUDA contexts, and some libraries that are not fork-safe, leading to hangs. `spawn` starts a fresh interpreter and imports the module, avoiding many fork-safety issues but often increasing startup overhead and requiring objects to be pickleable. In practice: move heavy initialization into the worker (inside `__getitem__`/worker init), avoid creating CUDA contexts in the parent before forking, consider setting `torch.multiprocessing.set_start_method('spawn', force=True)` for safety when using CUDA or thread-heavy libs, and tune `persistent_workers`, `prefetch_factor`, and worker count to manage memory.
questions.explanation: This is a common ML-on-Linux issue: it looks like a Python problem but is deeply tied to Linux process semantics. Understanding COW and fork-safety helps prevent hangs and unexpected RSS growth, especially with tokenizers, OpenMP-backed libraries, and GPU initialization.

questions.question_text:
A training job runs in a container and is limited to 8 CPUs and 32 GB RAM. The job still crashes with errors like “too many open files” and sometimes runs far slower than expected.

Which explanation best ties together cgroups and ulimits in this scenario?
options: A. cgroups control CPU/RAM quotas; ulimits control per-process resource limits like file descriptors/threads; both can independently break ML workloads even if the other looks fine
B. ulimits override cgroups, so cgroups don’t matter for containers
C. cgroups only affect networking; ulimits only affect GPU memory
D. If CPU and RAM are limited, Linux automatically increases file descriptor limits to compensate
questions.explanation: cgroups enforce resource isolation at the group level (CPU shares/quotas, memory limits, sometimes I/O). `ulimit` (RLIMITs) applies per process (or per user/session) and commonly impacts ML systems via `nofile` (file descriptors), `nproc` (process/threads), and `memlock`. A container can have plenty of cgroup memory but still fail because it cannot open enough files (datasets, sockets) or spawn threads.

questions.question_text:
Your model server logs appear in `journalctl`, but the disk is slowly filling up on a long-running VM. You also want logs to be queryable and not lose important context.

Describe a practical Linux logging setup for an ML endpoint using (1) `journalctl` usage, (2) structured logs, and (3) log rotation/retention controls.
questions.correct_answer: (1) Use `journalctl -u <service> --since ...` for querying per-unit logs, and `journalctl -u <service> -f` for tailing; ensure the service logs to stdout/stderr so journald captures it. (2) Emit structured logs (e.g., JSON) including request_id/trace_id, model version, latency, batch size, GPU id, and error codes; keep PII/secrets out. (3) Control retention via journald settings like `SystemMaxUse=`, `SystemKeepFree=`, and `MaxRetentionSec=` in `journald.conf`, or forward logs to a centralized system. If using file logs, configure `logrotate` with size/time-based rotation and compression; ensure permissions and that the service reopens logs (or use copytruncate carefully).
questions.explanation: In ML services, logs are both a debugging tool and an operational risk (disk usage). Journald provides per-service indexing and querying, but needs retention limits. Structured logging makes it feasible to debug latency spikes and model/version issues. Rotation/retention prevents outages caused by full disks.

questions.question_text:
You deploy a model server as a `systemd` service on a Linux VM. The service starts successfully when you run the command manually, but fails under `systemd` because it can’t find the right Python environment and doesn’t have the expected environment variables.

What are two `systemd`-native ways to make the runtime environment reliable (without relying on `.bashrc`), and why are they preferred?
questions.correct_answer: Two common `systemd`-native approaches: (1) Use `ExecStart=` with an absolute path to the intended interpreter/binary (e.g., `/opt/venv/bin/python -m myserver`) and set `WorkingDirectory=`. (2) Use `Environment=` and/or `EnvironmentFile=` (with strict permissions) to define required environment variables; alternatively use a drop-in override via `systemctl edit`. These are preferred because `systemd` does not run interactive shells and won’t source `.bashrc`; explicit paths and environment configuration make the service reproducible, auditable, and consistent across reboots and users.
questions.explanation: ML endpoints frequently run as services. `systemd` launches processes with a clean, controlled environment. Relying on shell init files is brittle and can break after updates or when running under different users. `systemd` unit directives provide declarative, versionable configuration that matches production needs.

questions.question_text:
You suspect a production inference binary is failing due to a missing file or permission issue, but logs are unclear. You want a minimal, disciplined Linux debugging workflow.

Which approach is most appropriate?
options: A. Start with `strace -f` on every service in the fleet and keep it running permanently
B. Enable core dumps, reproduce the crash once, inspect with `gdb`/`coredumpctl`, and use `strace` briefly only if needed to confirm syscalls like `open()`/`connect()` failures
C. Rebuild the kernel with extra debug prints and reboot production
D. Only use Python-level logging; Linux tools are unnecessary for inference issues
questions.explanation: A good workflow is to first capture a reproducible failure with minimal overhead: core dumps (or `coredumpctl`) help diagnose crashes; targeted `strace` can confirm syscall-level errors (missing files, permission denied, DNS/connect failures). Running `strace` permanently is high overhead and noisy. Kernel rebuilds are rarely justified for typical ML endpoint issues.

questions.question_text:
You run an NVIDIA GPU container and see:

- `nvidia-smi` works on the host.
- Inside the container: `nvidia-smi` fails or CUDA apps say the driver is missing.

Conceptually, which statement is correct about how GPU support works with containers?
options: A. The container must include the NVIDIA kernel driver; the host driver is irrelevant
B. The host provides the NVIDIA kernel driver; the container needs compatible user-space CUDA libraries (or relies on host-mounted ones via NVIDIA Container Toolkit)
C. Docker automatically virtualizes the GPU without any host configuration
D. You must compile CUDA kernels into the Linux kernel for containers to see GPUs
questions.explanation: Containers share the host kernel, so the NVIDIA kernel driver must be installed on the host. The container typically provides user-space libraries (CUDA runtime, cuDNN, etc.) that must be compatible with the host driver. NVIDIA Container Toolkit (`--gpus all`) mounts the right device files and driver libraries into the container. Mismatch between host driver and container CUDA stack is a common cause of failures.

questions.question_text:
A training job on a Linux host gets killed with no Python traceback. `dmesg` shows lines like:

```
Out of memory: Killed process 12345 (python) total-vm:... anon-rss:...
Killed process 12345 (python) ...
```

Explain (1) what component made the kill decision, (2) what swap and page cache have to do with it, and (3) two practical mitigations an ML engineer can try without changing the model code.
questions.correct_answer: (1) The Linux kernel OOM killer made the decision (not Python). It triggers when the system cannot reclaim enough memory to satisfy allocations and chooses a process to kill based on heuristics (oom_score/adj, memory usage, etc.). (2) Swap extends virtual memory by paging anonymous memory to disk; if swap is disabled or too small, the kernel has fewer options before OOM. Page cache is file-backed cache; under memory pressure it can be reclaimed, but large file I/O (datasets, checkpoints) can still contribute to pressure and stalls. (3) Mitigations: reduce batch size / number of dataloader workers (reduces RAM), enable or increase swap (or zram), set memory limits and monitor (cgroups), reduce dataset caching/prefetching, move to memory-mapped reads, or adjust `vm.swappiness` and ensure adequate free disk for swap. Also consider setting `oom_score_adj` for critical processes or isolating jobs to avoid system-wide contention.
questions.explanation: In real incidents, ML jobs often disappear because the kernel terminates them under memory pressure. Understanding user-space vs kernel-space boundaries helps: Python can’t catch an OOM kill. Swap and reclaimable caches affect how long the system can avoid OOM. Practical mitigations often involve runtime settings (batch size, workers, swap/zram, cgroup limits) rather than rewriting the model.

questions.question_text:
You try to run a Python package that uses a native extension (e.g., `tokenizers`, `xgboost`, or a custom C++/CUDA op) and get:

```
ImportError: libstdc++.so.6: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

What is the most practical first step on Linux to diagnose which OS package you’re missing?
options: A. Run `pip install libstdc++` to install the missing shared object
B. Run `ldd <path-to-extension>.so` and then map the missing library to an OS package via `apt-file`/`yum provides`
C. Set `LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib` and rerun until it works
D. Recompile the Linux kernel with GCC support enabled
questions.explanation: `ldd` shows which shared libraries a binary/extension depends on and which are missing at runtime. Once you know the missing `.so`, you can identify the owning OS package using tools like `apt-file search libstdc++.so.6` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `yum provides */libstdc++.so.6` / `dnf provides` (RHEL/Fedora). `pip` typically cannot install system libraries, and blindly setting `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` can mask the real issue and create fragile deployments.

questions.question_text:
You’re setting up a Linux box to run training jobs that need access to an S3-compatible store. A teammate proposes putting the access key in a file `~/.env` and sourcing it in `.bashrc`. What is the best security hygiene approach for an AI/ML engineer on a shared system?

Consider: file permissions, process environment leakage, and operational practicality.
questions.correct_answer: Prefer short-lived credentials (e.g., instance/role-based auth or OIDC) over long-lived static keys. If a secret must exist on disk, store it in a dedicated secrets mechanism (e.g., systemd EnvironmentFile with strict permissions, a secrets manager agent, or root-owned config readable only by the service user). Avoid exporting secrets broadly in interactive shells or `.bashrc` because environment variables can leak via `ps e`, crash dumps, logs, child processes, or misconfigured debugging. Ensure the secret file is `chmod 600` and owned by the service user (or root with group restrictions), and avoid committing it to repos. For services, inject secrets at runtime (systemd drop-in, Kubernetes secrets, or vault agent) and rotate regularly.
questions.explanation: ML engineers often run jobs under shared accounts, in tmux, or via services. Putting secrets in `.bashrc` spreads them to every shell/process, increasing accidental exposure. Linux permissions (`600`) reduce casual reads, but the bigger risk is secret propagation through process environments and logs. The most robust pattern is eliminating static secrets (roles/identity), otherwise scope them to the minimal service and inject at runtime with tight permissions and rotation.

questions.question_text:
You need a reproducible Linux environment for training/inference. A teammate’s wheel built on Ubuntu 22.04 fails on an older cluster with errors like `GLIBC_2.34 not found` or CUDA driver/runtime mismatches.

What practices best prevent these issues for AI/ML engineers (not Linux specialists)?
options: A) Build against the oldest compatible glibc/base image you must support, prefer manylinux/conda-forge wheels when possible, pin CUDA/cuDNN versions to match driver compatibility, and use containers to freeze system libraries
B) Always compile everything with `-static` including glibc to avoid any runtime dependencies
C) Use `pip install --upgrade` on every run so the newest packages fix compatibility automatically
D) Copy `/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu` from your laptop into the cluster nodes to match your environment
questions.explanation: glibc is not forward-compatible: binaries built on newer glibc often won’t run on older systems. For ML, CUDA compatibility also depends on the host driver. Practical, interview-relevant mitigation is to standardize builds on a known base (often older), rely on compatible binary distributions (manylinux/conda-forge), pin versions, and containerize to control system-level dependencies while respecting GPU driver constraints.

questions.question_text: You run PyTorch with `DataLoader(num_workers>0)` inside a Docker container and see errors like `RuntimeError: DataLoader worker (pid ...) exited unexpectedly` and sometimes `OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device` pointing at shared memory. Which Linux resource is commonly the real bottleneck, and what is the most typical fix?
options: A) `/dev/shm` (shared memory) size is too small; increase it (e.g., Docker `--shm-size`, Kubernetes `emptyDir: medium: Memory`), or reduce worker count/prefetching
B) `/etc/hosts` is missing entries; add the worker PIDs to `/etc/hosts`
C) `ip_forward` is disabled; enable routing to fix multiprocessing
D) The inode table is full; run `fsck` to expand inodes
questions.explanation: PyTorch multiprocessing and tensor sharing can rely on POSIX shared memory (`/dev/shm`). In containers, `/dev/shm` is often small by default, causing ENOSPC-like failures and worker crashes. Increasing shared memory size or reducing the amount of prefetched/queued data typically resolves it. This ties directly to Linux’s process/memory model and IPC behavior.

questions.question_text: A training job in Kubernetes shows low CPU usage in `top`, but step time is high and logs suggest the process is being throttled. Which Linux/Kubernetes-aligned evidence best distinguishes CPU throttling due to cgroup limits from “true” CPU saturation?
options: A) Check cgroup CPU stats (e.g., `/sys/fs/cgroup/.../cpu.stat` for throttled time/periods) and compare with `kubectl describe pod` CPU limits; throttling can happen even when host CPUs are idle
B) Run `dig` on the pod IP; throttling is usually a DNS issue
C) Increase `ulimit -n`; CPU throttling is caused by file descriptor limits
D) Use `df -i`; inode exhaustion causes CPU throttling
questions.explanation: With cgroup CPU quotas, a process can be throttled even if the node has idle CPU—`top` may show low usage because the task is prevented from running. The authoritative signal is cgroup CPU throttling counters (throttled periods/time) and the configured CPU quota/limit. This is a common ML issue when dataloaders or preprocessing are CPU-bound but the container has a low CPU limit.

questions.question_text:
You are optimizing dataloader throughput on a multi-socket (NUMA) server. The GPUs are attached to NUMA node 1, but your dataloader workers often run on CPUs from node 0, causing cross-socket traffic.

Describe how you would (a) check NUMA topology and current CPU/memory locality, and (b) pin the training process/dataloader to the desired NUMA node using Linux tools.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Check topology with `lscpu` (NUMA nodes), `numactl --hardware`, and optionally `nvidia-smi topo -m` for GPU locality. Inspect process placement with `ps -o pid,psr,comm -p <pid>` and CPU affinity with `taskset -pc <pid>`. For memory locality, `numastat -p <pid>` can help. (b) Pin using `numactl --cpunodebind=1 --membind=1 <command>` (or `--physcpubind`), or set CPU affinity via `taskset`/cgroups cpuset; ensure dataloader workers inherit affinity or explicitly set it per worker. If using PyTorch, combine with appropriate `OMP_NUM_THREADS` and worker counts to avoid oversubscription.
questions.explanation: On NUMA machines, remote memory access and cross-socket interconnect traffic can bottleneck input pipelines. Linux provides visibility (`lscpu`, `numactl --hardware`, `numastat`) and control (`numactl`, `taskset`, cpuset cgroups) to keep CPU threads and memory allocations close to the GPU’s PCIe root complex for better throughput and lower latency.

questions.question_text:
In a shared GPU server, your training job writes checkpoints to a directory that is bind-mounted into a container. Inside the container (running as non-root), you get `PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied` when writing, but the directory is writable on the host.

Which explanation is most accurate, and what’s a typical fix?
options: A) UID/GID mismatch between host and container for the bind mount; fix by running the container with matching `--user`, adjusting ownership/ACLs on the host, or using a shared group + correct permissions/umask
B) The filesystem is corrupted; fix by running `fsck` inside the container
C) Linux forbids bind mounts for ML workloads; fix by copying the dataset into the image layer
D) The error is caused by DNS; fix by editing `/etc/hosts`
questions.explanation: Bind mounts preserve host ownership and permission bits. If the container’s user has a different UID/GID than the host owner, writes can fail even though it “works on the host” for a different user. Common fixes: run the container with the same UID/GID (`--user`), change host directory ownership, use a shared group with `chmod g+rwX` + setgid bit, or set an appropriate `umask` so new files are group-writable.

questions.question_text:
A long-running training job fails during checkpoint saving with `OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device` even though `df -h` shows plenty of free space on the main filesystem.

Name two Linux causes that can still produce ENOSPC in this situation, and show one command to confirm each cause.
questions.correct_answer: Two common causes: (1) Inode exhaustion: lots of small files can consume all inodes even if bytes are free. Confirm with `df -i` (look for 100% IUse%). (2) The job writes to a different filesystem than you checked (e.g., `/tmp`, `/dev/shm`, a container overlay, or a mounted volume with its own capacity/quota). Confirm by checking the actual target path with `df -h <checkpoint_dir>` and inspecting mounts via `mount` or `findmnt`. Other valid causes: user/project quota exceeded (confirm with `quota -s` where applicable) or a full container writable layer (confirm with `df -h /` inside container and runtime storage info).
questions.explanation: ENOSPC can mean “no blocks” or “no inodes” or “quota exceeded,” and in containers it can also mean the overlay layer is full even if the host has space. The key is to check the filesystem that actually backs the path you’re writing to and to check inode usage, not just byte usage.

questions.question_text: You suspect a file-descriptor leak in a Python dataset implementation. The training process PID is 12345. Which command best lists the number and types of file descriptors currently open by that process (including sockets and regular files) on Linux?
options: A) `lsof -p 12345`
C) `ip link show 12345`
D) `traceroute 12345`
questions.explanation: `lsof -p <pid>` enumerates open files for a process, including regular files, pipes, and network sockets. This is a standard way to diagnose FD leaks and understand what resources a dataloader/training loop is holding open.

questions.question_text:
Your dataloader intermittently crashes on a large image dataset with: `OSError: [Errno 24] Too many open files`. You see it more often when `num_workers` is high.

What is the most effective immediate mitigation + diagnosis combo on Linux?
options: A) Increase the open-file limit with `ulimit -n` (or container ulimit), and use `lsof -p <pid>` (or `/proc/<pid>/fd`) to confirm FD growth and identify what’s open
B) Increase swap space and rerun; `Too many open files` is usually a RAM issue
C) Delete the dataset cache; this error only happens when disk is full
D) Disable TLS verification for downloads; certificate errors can show up as open-file errors
questions.explanation: Errno 24 is specifically the per-process file descriptor limit. Raising `ulimit -n` (and ensuring the container runtime allows it) can stop immediate failures, while `lsof` or `/proc/<pid>/fd` shows whether workers leak descriptors (e.g., not closing files) or legitimately need more. Higher `num_workers` increases concurrent open files/sockets/pipes, making FD limits a common ML failure mode.

questions.question_text: A containerized PyTorch job is failing with `socket.gaierror: [Errno -2] Name or service not known` when trying to reach `s3.mycompany.internal`. Which sequence is the best Linux-first debugging approach (prefer `dig`/`nslookup`, `/etc/hosts`, and interface/routing checks)?
options: A) Check `/etc/hosts` for an override, run `dig s3.mycompany.internal` (or `nslookup`), confirm `/etc/resolv.conf` nameservers, then verify network path with `ip addr` and `ip route`
B) Restart the container, reinstall Python, and retry; if it still fails, assume the service is down
C) Use `netstat -rn` to check routes and ignore DNS because `gaierror` indicates a TCP problem
D) Immediately add a hard-coded IP to the training code and skip DNS to avoid the issue
questions.explanation: `gaierror` is a name resolution failure, so start with DNS and host overrides: `/etc/hosts` can shadow DNS; `dig`/`nslookup` show whether the name resolves and to what; `/etc/resolv.conf` shows which resolvers the container uses. Only after resolution works should you validate interface/IP and routing with `ip addr`/`ip route`. The other options either ignore DNS or introduce brittle workarounds.

questions.question_text:
You’re debugging a training job that fails to download a model artifact from an internal endpoint: `https://model-registry.corp:8443/v1/models`. From inside the container, `curl -v` returns one of these outcomes. Match each outcome to the most likely root cause category and name one Linux command you’d use next to confirm.

Outcomes:
1) `connect to 10.12.0.8 port 8443 failed: Connection refused`
2) `connect to 10.12.0.8 port 8443 failed: Operation timed out`
3) `TLS handshake failed` / `SSL: certificate verify failed`

Provide your answer as a short mapping (1/2/3) -> (cause, next command).
questions.correct_answer: 1 -> (Service/port not listening on destination or actively rejected by host firewall; confirm with `ss -lntp` on server side if you have access, or `ss -tn state syn-sent`/`nc -vz` from client; also `ip route` to ensure correct path). 2 -> (Network path issue: routing, security group/NACL, firewall dropping, or host unreachable; confirm with `ip route get 10.12.0.8`, `traceroute`/`mtr 10.12.0.8`, and `ss -tn` to see SYN retries). 3 -> (TLS/cert/hostname/SNI mismatch or missing CA bundle; confirm with `openssl s_client -connect model-registry.corp:8443 -servername model-registry.corp` and DNS/hostname checks via `dig` plus verifying container CA certs).
questions.explanation: “Connection refused” means the TCP handshake reached the host and the host replied with RST—typically nothing is listening on that port or an active reject rule exists. “Timeout” means you didn’t get a response to SYN packets—often routing/firewall drops or the host is unreachable. TLS errors happen after TCP connect succeeds: the failure is at certificate validation, protocol/cipher negotiation, or hostname/SNI mismatch. Using `ss` helps distinguish connection states; `ip route`/`mtr` validate reachability; `openssl s_client` isolates TLS from application logic.

questions.question_text:
You're running a long training job and want to ensure it continues even if your SSH connection drops. You also need to monitor GPU memory and utilization continuously. Which combination of commands achieves this?

```bash
# Goal: 
# 1. Training survives SSH disconnect
# 2. Monitor GPU stats every 2 seconds to a log file
```
options: A) nohup python train.py & nvidia-smi -l 2 > gpu.log
B) tmux new -s training 'python train.py' && watch -n 2 nvidia-smi
C) tmux new -s training \; send-keys 'python train.py' Enter \; split-window 'nvidia-smi -l 2 | tee gpu.log'
D) screen -S training python train.py | nvidia-smi --loop=2
questions.explanation: Option C uses tmux to create a persistent session that survives SSH disconnection, runs training in one pane, and monitors GPU with `nvidia-smi -l 2` (refresh every 2 seconds) in another pane while logging with `tee`. Option A's `nohup` works but the nvidia-smi command would stop on disconnect. Option B's `watch` doesn't save to file. Option D's pipe syntax is incorrect. With tmux, you can detach with `Ctrl+b d` and reattach later with `tmux attach -t training`.

questions.question_text:
Your distributed PyTorch training across 4 GPUs using NCCL backend is failing with connection errors. Which Linux command helps verify that the required ports are open and the GPUs can communicate?

```
NCCL WARN Connect to rank 1 failed: Connection refused
```
options: A) ping localhost
B) ss -tlnp | grep python && nvidia-smi topo -m
C) netstat -r
D) ifconfig eth0
questions.explanation: `ss -tlnp | grep python` shows which ports your Python processes are listening on, helping identify if the rendezvous port is correctly bound. `nvidia-smi topo -m` shows GPU topology and NVLink/PCIe connectivity, which is crucial for NCCL performance. Together, these diagnose both network and GPU communication issues. `ping` (A) only tests basic connectivity. `netstat -r` (C) shows routing tables. `ifconfig` (D) shows interface configuration but not port status.

questions.question_text:
You need to create a systemd service to automatically start your ML inference API server on boot and restart it if it crashes. Complete the systemd unit file:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=ML Inference API Server
After=network.target

[Service]
User=mluser
WorkingDirectory=/home/mluser/inference-api
Environment="CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0"
ExecStart=_______________
Restart=_______________

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```
questions.explanation: For `ExecStart`, you should use the full path to the Python interpreter in your virtual environment to ensure correct dependencies. For `Restart`, use `always` or `on-failure` to ensure the service recovers from crashes. Other useful options include `RestartSec=5` (wait before restart) and `Environment` for setting paths like `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` for CUDA libraries. After creating the file in `/etc/systemd/system/ml-api.service`, run `sudo systemctl daemon-reload` and `sudo systemctl enable ml-api`.

questions.question_text:
You want to profile your ML training code to find CPU bottlenecks. The following `perf` command output shows your training process. What does this tell you about the bottleneck?

```
# perf top -p $(pgrep -f train.py)
  45.23%  libc-2.31.so       [.] __memmove_avx_unaligned_erms
  12.45%  libgomp.so.1.0.0   [.] GOMP_parallel
   8.32%  python3.9          [.] _PyEval_EvalFrameDefault
   6.21%  libtorch_cpu.so    [.] at::native::copy_
```
options: A) The Python interpreter is the main bottleneck
B) Memory copy operations are consuming the most CPU time
C) OpenMP parallelization overhead is the primary issue
D) PyTorch tensor operations are inefficient
questions.explanation: The `perf` output shows `__memmove_avx_unaligned_erms` consuming 45.23% of CPU time, indicating memory copy operations are the bottleneck. This often happens with excessive data copying in preprocessing, non-contiguous tensor operations, or inefficient data loading. Solutions include using `pin_memory=True`, ensuring tensors are contiguous, or reducing unnecessary copies. The Python interpreter (8.32%) and OpenMP (12.45%) are secondary concerns here.

questions.question_text:
You're setting up a multi-GPU training environment and need to make specific GPUs visible to your training script. The server has 8 GPUs but you only want to use GPUs 2, 3, and 5. How do you configure this before running your Python script?

```python
# Your training script uses:
import torch
model = torch.nn.DataParallel(model)
```
questions.explanation: Setting `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=2,3,5` before running your script makes only GPUs 2, 3, and 5 visible to CUDA. PyTorch will then see these as devices 0, 1, 2 (remapped indices). This is the standard way to control GPU allocation without modifying code. You can set it inline: `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=2,3,5 python train.py` or export it for the session. This is essential for sharing GPU servers with other users or isolating experiments.

questions.question_text: You need to verify that CUDA is properly installed and identify which GPUs are available for training. Which command provides the most comprehensive information about GPU availability, driver version, and CUDA version?
options: A) lspci | grep -i nvidia
B) nvidia-smi
C) cat /proc/driver/nvidia/version
D) nvcc --version
questions.explanation: `nvidia-smi` provides the most comprehensive view: GPU names, memory usage, driver version, CUDA version, running processes, and temperature. `lspci` (A) only shows hardware detection. `/proc/driver/nvidia/version` (C) only shows driver version. `nvcc --version` (D) shows the CUDA compiler toolkit version, which may differ from the driver's CUDA version. For ML setup verification, `nvidia-smi` is the go-to command.

questions.question_text:
After installing a new version of CUDA, your PyTorch script fails with:

```
OSError: libcudnn.so.8: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

Which approach correctly resolves this library path issue?
options: A) Run `pip install cudnn` to install the missing library
B) Add the cuDNN library path to `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` environment variable
C) Reinstall PyTorch with `pip install torch --force-reinstall`
D) Run `sudo apt install libcudnn8` without any path configuration
questions.explanation: The error indicates the dynamic linker can't find `libcudnn.so.8`. The solution is to add the cuDNN installation path to `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`: `export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/cuda/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH` (or wherever cuDNN is installed). You can make this permanent in `~/.bashrc`. Option A is wrong because cuDNN isn't a pip package. Option C won't help if the system library path is misconfigured. Option D might work but often cuDNN is installed manually and requires path configuration.

questions.question_text: You're working on a remote server and need to access Jupyter Lab running on port 8888 and TensorBoard on port 6006 from your local machine. The server only allows SSH access. Write the SSH command to set up port forwarding for both services.
questions.explanation: SSH local port forwarding (`-L`) creates a tunnel from your local machine to the remote server.
